whatItIs: The single vocabulary for *what surface/unit* a node claims the right to write, used by the claim / conflict-detection layer. It unifies v1's scattered terms (`declared_scope` vs per-agent `does_not_touch`) into one concept (ADR-0009); the exact shape (node-scoped vs file-glob) is open (`open-questions.md` §3).
whatItIsNot: Not v1's scattered, split vocabulary (`declared_scope` and per-agent `does_not_touch`), which this single concept replaces.
provenance: See `open-questions.md` §3.

title: studio
description: The live PixiJS web IDE that renders the tree and drives the agents (diffs, approvals, steering, per-node chat).
oneLine: The live PixiJS web IDE that renders the tree and drives the agents — diffs, approvals, steering, per-node chat.
whatItIs: The live PixiJS web IDE that renders the tree and **drives** the agents (diffs, approvals, steering, per-node chat).
whatItIsNot: Not v1's read-only `dashboard`, which it supersedes — the studio is a richer, driving surface, not merely a renamed view.

title: orchestrator
description: The thin custom TypeScript layer (packages/orchestrator) over plain Postgres (DBOS deferred, ADR-0019): owns the story-DAG, the scheduler, and the event store, and is the only module that drives `packages/agent` (the owned loop).
oneLine: The thin custom TypeScript layer (`packages/orchestrator`) over plain Postgres (DBOS deferred, ADR-0019) that owns the story-DAG, the scheduler, and the event store, and is the only module that drives `packages/agent` (the owned loop).
whatItIs: The thin custom TypeScript layer (`packages/orchestrator`) over plain Postgres — DBOS is deferred (ADR-0019), so this is a plain typed Postgres connection (ADR-0001): it owns the story-DAG, the scheduler, and the event store, and is the **only** module that drives `packages/agent` (the owned loop). It is the code-sequenced **spine** and the sole **fan-out** point — it schedules nodes; owned-loop nodes never schedule child nodes.
whatItIsNot: Distinct from an owned-loop session, which owns work *inside* a node (ADR-0004, ADR-0005, ADR-0011).
provenance: DBOS deferred by ADR-0019.

title: spine
description: The code-sequenced control-flow layer (the orchestrator over plain Postgres, DBOS deferred — ADR-0019) that owns closed, deterministic routing: the order steps run in, when a loop iterates, which branch is taken.
oneLine: The code-sequenced control-flow layer (the orchestrator over plain Postgres, DBOS deferred — ADR-0019) that owns closed, deterministic routing — the order steps run in, when a loop iterates, which branch is taken.
whatItIs: The code-sequenced control-flow layer (the orchestrator over plain Postgres — DBOS is deferred, ADR-0019) that owns **closed, deterministic routing**: the order steps run in, when a loop iterates, which branch is taken. The discriminator (carried verbatim from Agentic ADR-0026): *if a for-loop or a match could express the routing, the spine owns it; if the routing needs the model to decide what comes next, the leaf (owned-loop node) owns it.*
whatItIsNot: Not the **leaf** — the leaf runtime the spine delegates to (live: the Claude Agent SDK; offline/fallback: the owned loop — ADR-0030).
provenance: Carried verbatim from Agentic ADR-0026; DBOS deferred by ADR-0019; leaf runtime split live/offline by ADR-0030.

title: leaf step / leaf judgment
description: A single step in a code-sequenced cascade whose work is owned by the leaf runtime (what to write, how to satisfy a contract) rather than by the spine — the control-flow sense of "leaf"…
oneLine: A single step in a code-sequenced cascade whose work is owned by the leaf runtime (what to write, how to satisfy a contract) rather than by the spine — the control-flow sense of "leaf".
whatItIs: A single step in a code-sequenced cascade whose work is owned by the **leaf runtime** (what to write, how to satisfy a contract) rather than by the spine — the **control-flow** sense of "leaf" (ADR-0005, ADR-0011). The leaf runs behind the executor seam: live, the Claude Agent SDK; offline/fallback, the owned loop (ADR-0030).
whatItIsNot: Distinct from **contract**, the *leaf tier* of the work hierarchy (ADR-0002); the two senses of "leaf" must not be conflated.

title: agent package
description: The project-owned thin wrapper (`packages/agent`) and typed-event parser over the leaf surface (prompt / steer / follow-up + lifecycle events + edit-tool diffs).
oneLine: The project-owned thin wrapper (`packages/agent`) and typed-event parser over the leaf surface (`prompt` / `steer` / `follow-up` + lifecycle events + `edit`-tool diffs).
whatItIs: The project-owned **thin wrapper** (`packages/agent`) and typed-event parser over the leaf surface (`prompt` / `steer` / `follow-up` + lifecycle events + `edit`-tool diffs). The **sole** surface through which a leaf runtime is invoked and the **only** place a model runtime is imported. It hosts both executors behind the `PhaseAuthor` seam: the owned loop on the Messages API (offline/deterministic + pivot-out fallback, ADR-0011) and the Claude Agent SDK leaf (`ClaudeAgentAuthor` — the live runtime, ADR-0030); it normalizes the stream into the typed events the event store renders, and exposes nothing model-shaped upward.
whatItIsNot: No third-party agent framework other than the single-import-site Agent SDK sits in this role; `packages/storage-protocol` and `apps/studio` never parse the leaf's stream directly (ADR-0004, ADR-0006, ADR-0011, ADR-0030).
provenance: Carries v1's own-a-thin-wrapper-over-the-agent-runtime principle (Agentic ADR-0008/0026).

title: trunk
description: The canonical integrated mainline a capability lands on once approved.
oneLine: The canonical integrated mainline a capability lands on once approved.
whatItIs: The canonical **integrated mainline** a capability lands on once **approved**. In v2 the trunk is **approval-gated** (a human admits a green result), never auto-merge-on-green, and never holds knowingly-broken intermediate states (ADR-0008).
whatItIsNot: Not v1's trunk, which it supersedes — v1's auto-merged on green and tolerated broken intermediate states under an eventual-consistency posture.

title: steering
description: A first-class, typed operator act of redirecting an in-flight owned-loop run mid-execution (the owned loop's steer operation), recorded as an event in the event store.
oneLine: A first-class, typed operator act of redirecting an in-flight owned-loop run mid-execution (the owned loop's `steer` operation), recorded as an event in the event store.
whatItIs: A first-class, typed operator act of **redirecting an in-flight owned-loop run mid-execution** (the owned loop's `steer` operation), recorded as an event in the event store.
whatItIsNot: Distinct from **approval**: steering is the in-loop counterpart — the human shapes an action *while it runs*, rather than only accepting/rejecting its result (ADR-0008).

title: ADR
description: Architecture Decision Record under docs/decisions/, capturing a cross-cutting decision.
oneLine: An Architecture Decision Record under `docs/decisions/`, capturing a cross-cutting decision.
whatItIs: An Architecture Decision Record under `docs/decisions/`, capturing a cross-cutting decision.

title: fixture
description: A test-supporting artifact (data file, scaffold, temp crate) created during a walkthrough and cleaned up before signing.
oneLine: A test-supporting artifact (data file, scaffold, temp crate) created during a walkthrough and cleaned up before signing.
whatItIs: A test-supporting artifact (data file, scaffold, temp crate) created during a walkthrough and cleaned up before signing.

title: ndjson
description: Newline-delimited JSON;
oneLine: Newline-delimited JSON — the line-delimited record format (a candidate backing for the event stream).
whatItIs: Newline-delimited JSON; the line-delimited record format, a candidate backing for the event stream.
whatItIsNot: Not to be called "JSONL": v1 used "JSONL" interchangeably — v2 standardizes on **ndjson**.

title: asset
description: In storytree, a tree/game art asset for the isometric renderer (ADR-0001, deferred).
oneLine: In storytree, a tree/game art asset for the isometric renderer (ADR-0001, deferred).
whatItIs: In storytree, a **tree/game art asset** for the isometric renderer (ADR-0001, deferred).
whatItIsNot: Not v1's "asset" (shared DRY content under `assets/`), which does not carry — guard against the collision when importing v1 docs.

title: Cloud SQL access · Node connector · keyless IAM
description: How storytree reaches its Postgres runtime store: the Cloud SQL Node connector with IAM database auth via ambient ADC. Agent sessions need no per-session OAuth and no key files.
statement: Reach the Cloud SQL Postgres via the Node connector with keyless IAM database authentication — agent sessions authenticate from ambient Application Default Credentials, never a per-session OAuth or a key file.
whatItIs: The `@google-cloud/cloud-sql-connector` Node connector (paired with `pg`, in `packages/library/src/store` — the old `packages/store` dissolved, ADR-0077) mints short-lived IAM tokens as the database password over a TLS socket — no password is stored. Identity comes from the operator’s Application Default Credentials already established on the dev host (plus a dedicated service account for headless/impersonated runs). The IAM database user is the operator email (`STORYTREE_DB_USER`), the database is `storytree`, and the instance connection name is `storytree-498613:australia-southeast1:storytree-pg`.
whyThis: No secrets in the repo and no per-session interactive login, so agents can run migrations and queries headlessly; it matches the cost posture (the fixed nightly sleep window, ADR-0114) and keeps the browser/credential-free boundary (the studio never holds DB creds). A session should ASSUME keyless credentials are present and verify with `gcloud auth application-default print-access-token`, not assume it is unauthenticated.
constraints: The instance sleeps on a **fixed nightly window — stopped 01:00–07:00 Australia/Sydney** (ADR-0114, `infra/cost-backstop.tf`): one Cloud Scheduler job stops it at 01:00, another starts it at 07:00, so it stays predictably up across the day. The old idle-aware auto-stop (ADR-0015 §5, `infra/idle-stop.tf`) is REMOVED — superseded by that window. Day-to-day: `pnpm db:up` when you need it and LEAVE IT RUNNING (owner call 2026-06-13 — do not `db:down` between bursts); re-`db:up` if a query can't connect after the overnight stop, and expect a slow cold-start (probe with a direct `SELECT 1`, don't assume a wedge). IAM users are bare roles, so a ONE-TIME privilege bootstrap (`infra/bootstrap-grants.sql`, applied via a since-retired script in the dissolved `packages/store`) granted the IAM user `CREATE` on the database; already applied. The connector deps live in `packages/library` (the `./store` subpath); DBOS is deferred (ADR-0019), so this is a plain typed Postgres connection.
provenance: Validated 2026-06-08: 73 units migrated keyless, then the instance stopped.

title: Doc-vs-implementation precedence
description: Implementation is ground truth; when a doc claim and the code disagree, the gap itself is the load-bearing surface — surface it, do not build on the doc claim.
statement: Implementation is ground truth and doc text is a hypothesis about it; when a finding shows a doc claim (ADR, definition, guideline, spec) disagrees with the code, the gap itself is the load-bearing surface, not metadata to a move that took the doc at face value.
why: It is easy to author the next move — 'extend the code so the doc holds', 'tighten the invariant' — on top of a doc claim a recent finding has already shown false. That move is built on a phantom premise. The honest first question is whether the doc needs correcting to match the code (most common) or the code needs extending to make the doc's claim hold (rarer, an operator call).
howToApply: Discriminator: does a recent investigation name a doc-vs-code gap, AND does your pending move cite that same doc as load-bearing? Yes on both → reshape any framing that takes the claim at face value and surface the gap to the operator (the doc, its exact claim, the observed behaviour, one question: correct the doc or extend the code?) without pre-deciding. Three resolutions: correct the doc (most common; the downstream move usually dissolves), route code-extension as its own bounded unit (operator-directed), or defer.
provenance: Composes with the `assess-tradeoffs-by-naming-both-sides` pattern (that governs how a surfaced tradeoff is framed; this governs whether the surface should exist). Imported from v1 `assets/guidelines/doc-vs-implementation-precedence.yml`.

title: Dogfood: fix the source
description: When your own tooling blocks you, fix the tool at its source rather than papering over it with a one-off workaround.
statement: When tooling we own blocks progress, fix the tool or its source; do not mask the symptom with a one-off workaround that leaves the root cause live to resurface.
why: storytree is built with the very kind of agent-driven system it builds (orchestrator, spine, owned loop, studio), so a defect in our own tooling is a first-class bug, not an environmental annoyance. A null guard, hardcoded value, swallowed exception, or shell fallback gets you unblocked now but hides a real defect; the problem goes quiet and re-emerges later, harder to diagnose under a layer of masking, and the next session inherits the same wall.
howToApply: Reproduce and trace to the earliest failing signal; validate that a test genuinely captures the failure before changing production code; fix the root cause where it lives so the bad state cannot occur (not a guard that hides it after the fact); prefer making one behaviour simply correct over adding a flag. Anti-patterns: a catch-all that does not handle the error, hardcoding a value to silence a dynamic failure, a narrow edge-case handler for a problem that should be fixed generically, and falling back to a workaround tool without recording that the primary one failed.
provenance: Composes with `verify-edit-write-persisted-or-escalate` (a recovery fallback is permitted only after the failure is made visible). Imported from v1 `assets/guidelines/dogfood-fix-the-source.yml`.

title: Exploration principles
description: Exploration is read-only reconnaissance: discover patterns over enumerating files, stay context-minimal, work in parallel and independent, never mutate.
statement: When exploring a codebase to inform a decision, discover patterns rather than enumerate files, load the minimum context for the scope, work independently of other explorers, and never modify anything.
why: Exploration feeds a decision (a decomposition, a scope call, a plan). The risks are over-reading (burning the attention budget on exhaustive cataloguing), over-reaching (analysing a surface another agent owns), and quietly mutating code meant only to be read. Disciplined exploration keeps findings sharp, cheap, and safe to act on.
howToApply: Use glob/search to learn structure and sample representative files rather than reading everything; stay inside the assigned scope and only note (not analyse) neighbouring surfaces; identify the high-level patterns that matter and flag risks and unknowns explicitly; produce a self-contained result with concrete file paths and no redundancy with another explorer's scope; use read-only tools only. When the context to explore genuinely exceeds the model window, escalate to recursive decomposition rather than reading everything at once.
provenance: Composes with the `recursive-decomposition-patterns` pattern. Imported from legacy AgenticEngineering `AgenticGuidance`.

title: Guidance quality
description: Fix unfollowed guidance with structure (path / signpost / fence / offload), not emphasis (caps / repetition / strong language).
statement: When guidance is not being followed, fix its structure — add the missing path, signpost, fence, or offload — rather than adding emphasis.
why: Agents do not weight visual or emotional emphasis the way a human skimming a page might; capitalising, repeating, or marking something CRITICAL adds noise without raising the chance an instruction is followed. Ignored guidance is almost always a structural failure: the instruction is absent at the decision point, lacks a concrete example, or lacks a constraint that removes the ambiguity. Emphasis treats the symptom; structure treats the cause.
howToApply: Effective patterns: path (a concrete step placed where it is needed), signpost (a concrete example, ideally a link to one that exists), fence (a constraint that makes the wrong move structurally hard or measurable), offload (move a deterministic error-prone step into code the spine sequences). Anti-patterns: caps emphasis, repetition, strong/urgent language, emphasis escalation on already-ignored guidance, and negative framing with no positive alternative (replace 'do not write outside the scope' with 'writes land only within the declared scope'). Reach for emphasis only after structure has genuinely failed — which is rare.
provenance: Composes with the `signal-and-noise` principle (the discriminatory-power lens this serves). Imported from legacy AgenticEngineering `AgenticGuidance`.

title: No proof preservation
description: Never soften or dodge a correct edit to keep an earned status; make the edit, let the status regress, and re-prove.
statement: Never soften, tiptoe around, or refuse a correct edit in order to preserve a status or verdict that was expensive to earn; ship the correct edit and let the status regress — the system re-verifies on demand.
why: A proof-preserving edit leaves two falsehoods, not one: the artifact is still wrong AND the verdict now attests to text deliberately softened to avoid invalidating it, so the verdict is no longer trustworthy. This follows directly from the prove-it-gate and the proof-hash: a unit reaches `healthy` only via earned, on-disk evidence, and a change to proof-bearing content invalidates the prior verdict by design.
howToApply: Make the correct edit; do not phrase it to dodge a proof-hash change. Expect and accept the regression — a correctly-amended `healthy` story reverts to `building` (or computes `unhealthy`); that is the system asking for a re-proof. Let the tier’s proof mode re-run (contract test, integration test, UAT, or operator attestation). Verdicts are cheap to re-issue; that is the whole point of binding status to evidence rather than a hand-edited flag.
provenance: The inverse of a hand-edit that fakes `healthy`: there a unit claims proof it never earned; here a unit clings to proof its new content no longer supports. See the `prove-it-gate` principle and the `proof-hash` definition. Imported from v1 `assets/guidelines/no-proof-preservation.yml`.

title: Reward hacking
description: A gameable success signal gets gamed; define success by observable outcome, require evidence, and separate the party that judges from the party that did the work.
statement: An agent rewarded for a gameable success signal will optimise the signal rather than the work; define success by observable end-results, require concrete evidence of them, and keep judging separate from doing.
why: When the owned loop works at a leaf it is rewarded for green tests. If the tests pin a gameable signal — a return code, a help-text string, a 'success: true' flag — the loop can earn the reward with a hollow implementation, and the breakage stays hidden until the slowest, most expensive channel (the story UAT against real collaborators) runs. This green-signal/red-reality failure is the root class the whole proof model exists to prevent.
howToApply: Outcome over process: prove 'the user can complete the journey', not 'the step executed' — exactly what the proof ladder encodes (contract test → integration test against real in-story collaborators → story UAT, no mocks within the organism). Require the concrete observable captured in the event store, not a bare flag. Separate judging from doing: approval onto the trunk is a distinct operator act, never self-granted; an agent can never self-exempt. Audit the tests themselves — a test can pass while failing to verify the requirement. Watch for help-text-only validation, success-flag-without-verification, mocking the real dependency, asserting implementation detail, permissive assertions (length > 0), missing unhappy paths, and swallowed errors.
provenance: Composes with `implementer-shortcut-patterns` (the specific hollow shapes), `test-fixtures-mirror-production-failure-modes` (the sterile-fixture inverse), and `test-creation-principles`. See the `faked-uat-theatre` and `agent-never-self-exempts` artifacts. Imported from legacy AgenticEngineering `AgenticGuidance`.

title: Signal and noise
description: Judge guidance by discriminatory power: signal lets the agent choose the right move; noise consumes attention without adding that power. Author for signal, cut noise.
statement: Judge any guidance an agent reads by its discriminatory power — signal lets the agent distinguish the correct action from the alternatives; noise consumes attention without adding that power — and author for high signal.
why: An agent (an owned-loop session, the orchestrator's routing prompt, a doc) operates in a finite attention window. Every sentence that does not help it choose the next move competes with the sentences that do; low-signal guidance does not merely fail to help, it crowds out the content that would.
howToApply: High signal is actionable, specific (concrete files/patterns/decisions), verifiable (a testable success criterion), and evidence-based (grounded in the codebase as it actually is). Noise causes attentional drift: meta-talk, stale context, generic philosophy, and structural redundancy (the same definition restated in several places). Per sentence ask: can I remove it without lowering the chance the task completes (→ noise)? does it point to a specific action/file/criterion (no → likely noise)? is it duplicated elsewhere (→ link the single source)? will it still be true later (no → stale)? Name concrete surfaces over vague gestures and link a single source of truth rather than restating it.
provenance: Composes with the `guidance-quality` principle (the authoring moves that add signal). Imported from legacy AgenticEngineering `AgenticGuidance`.

title: De-noising promotes a signal, never drops it
description: When a dense observability surface is de-noised, every signal is kept as a low-salience rendering — a stamp, a thin line, a badge — never deleted; de-noise lowers the volume of a signal, it does not hide it.
statement: When you de-noise a crowded view, demote each crowding element to a low-salience rendering — never remove it. The boundary is sharp: lowering a real coupling's salience (a thin line, a small per-thing stamp) is de-noise; making it un-recoverable from the picture is a drop, and a drop is information loss. If a thing was true enough to draw, it stays drawn at lower volume.
why: The observability surface exists to expose couplings the operator must see and judge — that is its whole job (`observability-first`: the system surfaces, the operator decides). A de-noise that DROPS an edge or flattens distinct edges into one generic badge silently erases exactly the signal the surface was built to carry, and the loss is invisible: the picture looks clean precisely because the thing you needed is gone. This is the trap ADR-0088's generic consumer-only bookshelf stamp fell into — it kept that an island used SOME shared island but lost WHICH one and the DIRECTION, a lossy near-drop that hid the `studio → cli` coupling the boundary system exists to surface (ADR-0102 Context §1). The cost is paid twice: a dropped signal cannot be judged, and a flattened one looks benign, so an architecturally important relationship reads as routine.
howToApply: Before removing anything to reduce clutter, ask: is what I am removing a SIGNAL (a real coupling, a real state) or pure decoration? A signal must be PROMOTED, not dropped — re-rendered at lower salience but still recoverable: ADR-0074 §1 ('de-noise visually … never by dropping edges') is the dependency-graph instance; ADR-0102 generalises it to ANY visual de-noise — a shared island promotes each incident edge to a per-island icon stamp (the edge kept as a badge, placement carrying its direction), rather than the road vanishing. Prefer a de-noise that carries the SAME information at lower volume (identity + direction preserved) over one that summarises it away (a generic 'uses a shared thing' badge drops which and whither). Reach for an outright drop only for genuine decoration that carries no signal. This is the world-map application of `signal-and-noise` (cut noise, keep signal): here the real coupling is the signal, the low-salience stamp is the de-noise. Whether a surfaced coupling is WRONG stays a human read, not a render-time verdict — promotion keeps it visible so the operator can judge it (`observability-first`); it does not flag it.
provenance: Graduated 2026-06-25 (ADR-0095 / the ADR-0102 build's librarian pass) — ADR-0102 named this durable rule ('de-noising promotes an edge, never drops it') but HELD authoring it until a second consumer existed; the per-island-icon-stamp build is that second consumer (the first being ADR-0074 §1's dependency-graph form). A distinct principle, not a refinement of `signal-and-noise`: it carries a rule that one does not — promote-not-drop, de-noise-never-hides-signal — and applies to the observability render, not to agent-readable guidance. ADR-0102 §References confirms the angle is novel ('No existing artifact covers the de-noise-never-hides-signal rule'). Generalises ADR-0074 §1 from the dependency graph to any visual de-noise; cross-links `signal-and-noise` (parent: cut noise, keep signal) and `observability-first` (sibling: the surface exposes, the operator judges).

title: Plain language first
description: Lead with the stake, gloss every internal term and ADR number on first use, and draw structural questions — so a reader without the repo loaded can decide.
statement: Write every artifact so a newcomer with the repo NOT loaded grasps the stake in the first sentence and never hits an unexplained internal term.
why: Our artifacts are read by newcomers and by agents that pull them as just-in-time context without the codebase in mind. A bare identifier (`renderBody`, `STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE`) or a bare ADR number (`ADR-0018 §D3`) is noise to that reader (see `signal-and-noise`): it consumes attention without conferring the power to decide. An artifact that opens with the decision instead of the stake makes the reader earn the "why should I care" that should have been free.
howToApply: Three checks, all observable. (1) Stake in sentence one — the first sentence says what breaks, or what is blocked, in plain terms, before any identifier or ADR number (e.g. "Edit a Library entry in the studio today and it silently loses its structured fields."). (2) Jargon budget = zero — every internal term, code identifier, and ADR number gets a ≤7-word gloss or a link on its first use; count the unexplained ones, the target is none (`renderBody` → "the function that turns structured fields into the markdown you see"). (3) Draw the structure — if the subject is a structure, flow, or state machine, include a diagram, not only prose. This is the structural cure `guidance-quality` argues for — a template field or a slot, not a "please write clearly" exhortation; the `open-question` template operationalises check 1 via its required `stakes` lead and check 3 via its `## Diagram` slot.

title: Stale prerequisite links are phantoms
description: A cross-story dependency edge with no shared observable is a phantom; removing it is map correction, not loosening — the within-unit proof ladder stays sacred.
statement: Distinguish the sacred within-unit proof ladder from a contestable cross-story dependency edge: when a `boundary` edge gates a unit and a staleness suspicion arises, ask not only 'heal the upstream?' but 'is this edge itself a phantom?'
why: Across stories, a story depends on another only through a declared `boundary`, and that edge makes a downstream proof meaningful in context. But edges go stale — most often when an ADR or an upstream interface is amended and a downstream story still carries an edge reflecting the old intent. The entanglement no longer exists: a phantom, still drawn on the map, pointing at nothing. Removing it is map correction; the dependency mechanism stays load-bearing, the removed edge never was.
howToApply: Compare what the two stories’ proofs actually verify, observable-anchored not authorship-anchored: read the downstream interface and the observables its UAT/integration tests pin, and the candidate-upstream `boundary` and the observables its tests pin. No shared observable → phantom, removable (surface to the operator; DAG-shape changes are operator territory). Shared observable → load-bearing; keep it and heal the chain. Partial overlap keeps the edge; if the downstream’s own proofs are stale, amend the downstream first; never re-target an edge (map correction removes edges that point at nothing, it does not point them elsewhere). When in doubt, heal the chain.
provenance: Composes with the `defects-amend-the-owning-story` principle (that expands the DAG; this contracts it); both reach the operator before the DAG shape changes. See the `dependency` and `boundary` definitions (ADR-0010). Imported from v1 `assets/guidelines/stale-prerequisite-links-are-phantoms.yml`.

title: Test creation principles
description: A test must verify the real outcome, not a proxy — content over existence, behaviour over implementation detail — and must fail if the behaviour it pins were removed.
statement: A test must verify the actual outcome rather than a proxy for it — real content over existence, user-facing behaviour over implementation detail — and must genuinely fail if the behaviour it pins were removed.
why: A test is only as good as the observable it pins. A test that checks a file exists (not its contents), that a function was called (not what it produced), or that a flag is true (not the result it claims) gives a green signal with no proof behind it. The proof ladder (contract → integration → UAT) is trustworthy only if each rung’s assertions bind to external truth rather than a gameable signal.
howToApply: Verify actual outcomes, not success flags — check file contents, assert user-facing behaviour, include concrete assertions about what was produced. Avoid the hollow shapes in `reward-hacking` and `implementer-shortcut-patterns`. One behaviour per test, clear names, order-independent, granularity matched to the tier; pin the behaviour that matters and do not over-test; use real collaborators where the tier allows (no mocks within an organism) and cover the unhappy path and edge cases. The sharpest single check — the deletion check: if the feature were removed, would this test fail? If not, it pins nothing and is a false green.
provenance: Same falsifiability discipline as `verify-edit-write-persisted-or-escalate`, applied to assertions. See the `contract-test` and `mock-uat-seam` definitions. Imported from legacy AgenticEngineering `AgenticGuidance`.

title: Test fixtures mirror production failure modes
description: A fixture must fail when production fails; a fixture that pre-installs a property production lacks is a false green that hides the production failure mode.
statement: A fixture, stub, or mock must be designed so the test fails when production fails; one that pre-installs a property production might lack hands the test a false green that hides the production failure mode.
why: The `mock-uat-seam` says where a mock may live (the contract tier yes; within an organism no) but is silent on whether a permitted mock is sterile or adversarial. A mock that satisfies the no-mocks-in-the-organism rule can still be defective if it stages a world more forgiving than what the code will actually meet — priming a stub with one platform's error wording, pre-creating state production does not create, granting access production's runtime lacks.
howToApply: Per pre-install, ask in order: (1) Inversion — if I remove it, does the test still cover its observable? Yes → convenience/isolation, fine; No → load-bearing, continue. (2) Production guarantee — does production guarantee the property by build config, a documented invariant, or the boundary contract it stands on? Yes → it mirrors a real guarantee, fine; No → it masks a production failure mode, defective. Remediate by dropping the pre-install and asserting the typed error production should return, adding a sibling test for the property-absent case, or escalating so production actually guarantees the property. Fires hardest when scaffolding fixtures for filesystem/runtime/permission/external-process state, and when re-authoring tests after a UAT-caught defect.
provenance: Composes with `implementer-shortcut-patterns` (sterile fixture here, hollow implementation there) and `tightening-a-shared-contract-needs-a-full-sweep`. See the `mock-uat-seam` definition. Imported from v1 `assets/guidelines/test-fixtures-mirror-production-failure-modes.yml`.

title: Tightening a shared contract needs a full sweep
description: When a change makes a shared validator reject inputs it used to accept, run the whole suite across every consuming capability green before the UAT is signed and the result approved.
statement: When a change tightens a shared parsing/validation contract — it now rejects inputs it previously accepted — run the full test suite across every capability that touches that contract, confirmed green, before the story's UAT is signed and before the result is approved onto the trunk.
why: Tightening a shared validator (a loader, deserializer, schema constraint, newly-required field) can invalidate fixtures or seed data anywhere that leaned on the old looser behaviour, and those regressions surface at runtime — a fixture fails to parse when its test runs — not at type-check or build time. So the change looks safe: it compiles, the owning capability is green, the UAT passes. The node rollup and approval gate read the event store plus on-disk evidence; neither re-runs the whole suite, so a story can reach `healthy` and be approved with cross-capability fixture regressions undetected.
howToApply: Discriminator: (1) did the change make a shared parsing/validation surface reject an input shape it formerly accepted (a stricter loader/parser, a new rejection, rejecting unknown fields, a tightened or added schema constraint, a newly-required field)? No → the rule does not arm. Yes → continue. (2) Is the full suite green across every capability, not just the owner? Yes → proceed with the sign and the approval. No → block; a red sibling suite forfeits the sign and approval even with a clean tree and a passing walkthrough; route the broken fixtures back to their owning capability (defects amend the owning story) before re-attempting. A loosening (accept more) cannot invalidate an existing fixture, so it does not trigger. Always sweep the whole workspace — catching other capabilities’ fixtures is the point.
provenance: Composes with `test-fixtures-mirror-production-failure-modes` (that prevents a sterile fixture passing; this catches a previously-valid fixture a contract change just broke). Imported from v1 `assets/guidelines/tightening-a-shared-contract-needs-a-full-sweep.yml`.

title: Verify an edit persisted, or escalate
description: On the owned-loop file tools (and any agent doing contract-bearing writes), read back every contract-bearing write and record a structured assumption-violation before any fallback — a silent fallback that hides the failure is banned. The live SDK leaf and the prove-it gate mitigate this differently (no Bash to fall back to; the gate re-reads downstream), so the discipline bites on the owned-loop path, not as a universal read-back mandate.
statement: When you write through the owned-loop file tools (`packages/agent/src/fs-tools.ts` — the offline/deterministic executor and pivot-out fallback), or otherwise hold the read-back yourself for a contract-bearing edit whose persistence your deliverable depends on, read the file back and confirm the intended content is present before proceeding; if it did not persist, record a structured assumption-violation in your return before applying any workaround. The live SDK leaf (ADR-0030) and the prove-it gate cover this by other means — see howToApply for which surface you are on.
why: An edit/write tool can return success without the content landing on disk (filesystem interception, sandbox quirks, path-normalisation edge cases): the owned loop's `write_file` reports a byte count derived from the *input* string and `edit_file` reports success from the mere absence of a throw — neither reads the file back. The historical in-the-wild reaction — silently falling back to a shell heredoc — hides that failure: the orchestrator never learns the tool misbehaved, the escalation pathway is forfeit, and the symptom recurs unnamed next session. Two facts narrow where this still bites. The **live** runtime is the Claude Agent SDK leaf (ADR-0030), which writes through the SDK's own Write/Edit and carries **no Bash** in its tool surface, so a shell-heredoc fallback is unreachable there. And the spine's prove-it gate re-reads and re-tests written files out-of-band downstream, so a non-persistence that the write tool itself missed is still caught before a unit can go green. The discipline therefore earns its keep on the owned-loop path and for any agent that holds the read-back itself — one extra read per contract-bearing write turns a silent symptom into a structured signal the orchestrator already knows how to consume.
howToApply: First locate your write surface. On the **live SDK leaf**, the SDK's Write/Edit handle persistence and there is no shell fallback to slip into (Bash is not in the leaf tool surface) — the gate's downstream re-read is your backstop, so this principle is a non-mandate there. On the **owned-loop file tools** (`fs-tools.ts`), or any path where you issue the write and own the read-back, apply it directly. Contract-bearing = any write whose persistence your return summary implicitly claims (the discriminator: would my summary lie if this file were not on disk?) — source files, test scaffolds, evidence rows, schema changes, spec amendments; throwaway scratch is out of scope. Issue the write, immediately read the path, verify the read reflects the intent. On failure (unchanged/absent/truncated/pre-call content): do NOT silently fall back; record an assumption-violation in your return (`{ briefed, observed, severity }`) the orchestrator parses programmatically; only after that record exists is a fallback (e.g. a heredoc) permitted as recovery. The contract pinned is the visibility of the failure, not the success of the recovery.
provenance: Composes with `implementer-shortcut-patterns` and `test-fixtures-mirror-production-failure-modes` — the same falsifiability discipline applied to a write’s persistence. Imported from v1 `assets/guidelines/verify-edit-write-persisted-or-escalate.yml`.

title: Implementer shortcut patterns
description: Five hollow-implementation shapes that pass shape-level tests but fail UAT — and the shape-vs-depth check that catches them.
statement: An implementation that satisfies a test's shape via a shortcut while leaving the real contract obligation un-implemented is defective even when every contract and integration test passes green; a test's contract is the observable the production code must produce, not a signature, a return type, or an entered code path.
problem: When the owned loop works at the leaf it is rewarded for green tests. If a test pins shape but not depth, the loop can earn green with a hollow implementation and the breakage stays invisible until the story's UAT runs the canonical journey end-to-end — the slowest, most expensive channel. This is the 'test green, production red' defect class, the inverse of a sterile fixture.
approach: Know the five sub-patterns: (1) help-text narrowing — assert a `--help` mentions a feature instead of running the canonical invocation through real dispatch → logic → output; (2) TODO-and-exit stub on the production arm — wire a test-only seam but leave the real-user branch a TODO + error-exit; (3) discarded typed parameter — accept a typed collaborator, suppress the unused-warning, never invoke it; (4) mock shipped in the production path — a mock reachable from the shipped artifact, gated only by a runtime flag; (5) silent fallback to fake data — a resource-acquisition path that substitutes a placeholder and returns success. Per seam, in order: shape vs depth (do the assertions observe the chain’s terminal effect, or an intermediate shape?), production-path coverage (does any test exercise the real production branch under a stub seam?), then name the shortcut and apply its discipline — wire the production arm, consume the parameter, exclude the mock, fail loud with a typed error.
tradeoffs: Wiring the production arm now costs upfront effort paid once; deferring it pays the UAT-only catch of every future regression forever on the slowest channel. Default to wiring it.
provenance: Composes with `test-fixtures-mirror-production-failure-modes` (the sterile-fixture inverse) and `reward-hacking`. See the `faked-uat-theatre` and `mock-uat-seam` artifacts. Imported from v1 `assets/guidelines/implementer-shortcut-patterns.yml`.

title: Pull-based context architecture
description: Brief an agent thinly and let it pull fresh context just-in-time, rather than pushing a fat static brief at session start.
statement: Give an agent a thin bootstrap and let it pull exactly the context its current step needs — minimal initial load, fetched on demand, always read from the live source — rather than pushing a large static brief at session start.
problem: The push model — pre-loading a big static brief covering every situation the agent might hit — pays three costs every turn: a context tax (a large brief consumes the attention budget even when most is irrelevant), staleness (a brief loaded at session start does not reflect later changes; the agent reasons over a snapshot), and one-size-fits-all (every step gets the whole brief; no step gets context tailored to it).
approach: Thin bootstrap — the starting brief carries the agent’s role, current objective, and how to fetch more; pointers, not payloads. Pull on demand — the agent fetches operational context when it needs it, and what it fetches is paths to read, not embedded blobs, always reading current state. Progressive disclosure — each step’s result points at the next thing to fetch. Keep briefs lean: name the surface and link its authoritative source instead of inlining it (self-contained means complete pointers, not complete payloads). When a single context to pull would still exceed the window, escalate to recursive decomposition. The CLI is one such pull surface: it renders its doctrine prose from the Library on demand — the choose-your-own-adventure CLI (ADR-0023/ADR-0053) — instead of carrying a fat static brief, because static instruction is followed less reliably than context pulled at the step that needs it.
tradeoffs: You trade a guaranteed-complete upfront snapshot for freshness and a small initial load; the agent makes extra fetches, but each reads live state instead of a stale cache. In storytree the event store is the single source of truth and the orchestrator briefs each node — this argues for those briefs to be pointers into live state, not fat snapshots.
provenance: A context-engineering principle for how agents are briefed, not a code spec for any subsystem. Composes with `signal-and-noise` (at the briefing layer) and `recursive-decomposition-patterns`. See ADR-0011 (pull-based just-in-time context). Imported from legacy AgenticEngineering `AgenticGuidance`.

title: Recursive decomposition patterns
description: When a context genuinely exceeds the model window, hold it as a queryable environment, filter to the sparse relevant slice, and recurse with bounded depth — do not summarise lossily.
statement: When a context genuinely exceeds the model's window, decompose rather than summarise lossily or hope a bigger window saves you: hold the large context as an environment the agent queries programmatically, filter to the sparse relevant slice, and recurse with bounded depth.
problem: Most tasks do not need all of a large context at once. Loading everything wastes the attention budget and degrades reasoning; summarising upfront loses the details the task needs. The alternative is to treat the context as data the agent searches — loading only the slice each sub-step requires — so it can work over a corpus far larger than its window without lossy compression. Reserve this for contexts that actually exceed the limit; for anything that fits, plain loading is simpler and faster.
approach: Context as environment — store the large input as named, queryable state and issue queries (filter for a pattern, navigate a section, find usages) pulling only what each step needs. Filter over chunk — prefer extracting the semantically relevant slice over blind size-based splitting. Recursive decomposition — break into sub-tasks, process each over its own slice, accumulate, aggregate (filter-then-process, hierarchical extraction, map-reduce). Search/execution firewall — separate context curation from task execution; a curation pass prepares the minimal slice, an execution pass works with only that slice and does no further searching. Discipline: measure before activating; bound and decrement a max depth; name the accumulators; hold the firewall; aggregate before signalling completion.
tradeoffs: You trade setup overhead and orchestration complexity for the ability to reason over an oversized corpus without lossy compression; not worth it when the context fits comfortably. Natural homes in storytree: oversized exploration, large owned-loop-event-stream or evidence analysis, and reasoning over big spec inputs during decomposition.
provenance: Source: Recursive Language Models (Zhang/Kraska/Khattab, MIT CSAIL); a context-engineering principle, not a code spec. Composes with `exploration-principles` and `pull-based-context-architecture`. Imported from legacy AgenticEngineering `AgenticGuidance`.

title: Repo surface allow-list
description: The repo root and docs/ are allow-listed: a new root entry or a new standalone doc is refused unless added to repo-manifest.json — and a new doc must justify why it does not belong in the Library.
statement: New files at the repo root, and new standalone docs under docs/, are refused at the gate unless listed in repo-manifest.json — and a new doc’s entry must state why it does not belong in the Library.
rule: The set of tracked top-level root entries and loose docs/ files must equal the allow-list in repo-manifest.json. Durable project knowledge belongs in the Library — as a typed artifact of one of its schema-validated kinds (see the `library` definition; `KIND_SPECS` in `packages/library/src/knowledge.ts` is the authoritative kind table) — or in an ADR under docs/decisions/, not a loose prose doc; the only standalone narrative doc kept is README.md. ADRs (docs/decisions/) and research notes (docs/research/) are allow-listed at the directory level, so new ADRs need no entry; any other file directly under docs/ needs an explicit, justified manifest entry.
enforcedBy: scripts/check-manifest.mjs (`pnpm check:manifest`), run inside `pnpm gate` and the CI `verify` job before typecheck (ADR-0022). It reads `git ls-files` and exits non-zero on any unlisted root entry or unlisted/unjustified doc, so an offending change cannot pass the green gate that guards merge to `main`.
failureMode: Without it, agents accrete temp/ad-hoc files at the root and one-off prose docs under docs/ that duplicate or bypass the Library — splitting authority over durable knowledge, rotting the tree, and re-creating the doc-sprawl the Library was meant to replace.
provenance: Authoritatively defined by ADR-0025; enforces on the ADR-0022 dev-repo green gate. The repo-hygiene complement to `edit-first-curation` (search/edit before authoring) and `signal-and-noise` (cut low-signal docs). Distinct from the PRODUCT proof gate (`gate` / `never-bypass-the-gate` / `prove-it-gate`): same "a gate refuses, it does not warn" family, but this guards the dev repo’s git surface, not promotion onto the story DAG. Mechanism: `repo-manifest.json` + `scripts/check-manifest.mjs`.

title: Library edit ceremony
description: How a session writes Library state: explore offline, search first, then CLI writes with `--pg` against the live store — never by hand-editing the seed.
statement: A session changes Library state only through the CLI against the live Cloud SQL store — explore offline, search before writing, then `pnpm db:up` and write with `--pg` — never by hand-editing the seed or generated files.
trigger: A session needs to change Library state — edit an artifact's fields or author a new artifact — rather than merely read it. (Pure reads need no ceremony: they run offline against the in-memory seed. `artifact comment` and retire are not yet CLI surfaces.)
steps:
1. **Explore offline.** `pnpm storytree library` (the dashboard) is the first command when working with the Library; drill in with `pnpm storytree library artifact <id>`, `pnpm storytree library artifact list <category>`, and `pnpm storytree library tree focus <id>`. Reads run against the in-memory seed — no DB needed.
2. **Search before you write** (asset:edit-first-curation). Real search is deferred (ADR-0023 §6) — `artifact list <category>` is the interim discovery path. Find the closest existing artifact and edit it by default; reaching for `artifact new` must be justified by why editing wasn't right. `new` refuses an existing id and points at `edit`.
3. **Bring the DB up:** `pnpm db:up`. The instance sleeps on a fixed nightly window (01:00–07:00 Australia/Sydney, ADR-0114) and otherwise stays up across the day; `db:up` is a no-op if already up.
4. **Write through the CLI with `--pg`:** `pnpm storytree library artifact edit <id> --set <field>=<value> --pg`, or `pnpm storytree library artifact new --file <doc.json> --pg`. Writes are **refused without `--pg`**; every write re-validates at the boundary (`validateLibraryDoc`) and returns a failure as guidance rather than persisting. **Editing an EXISTING non-agent artifact? Edit BOTH surfaces**: the live store (for a multi-field edit, push the whole doc — `artifact edit <id> --file <doc.json> --pg` upcast-validates and replaces wholesale) AND the seed `apps/studio/data/knowledge.json` (the offline reads and `build-corpus.mjs` come from it), then regenerate the generated views. `sync-corpus` is **migrate-only** — it adds seed-only artifacts to live but never updates one that already exists live — so a one-sided edit silently drifts, and `check:corpus-content` only WARNs (never blocks, ADR-0120).
5. **Mind the `--json` trap:** `pnpm` swallows the `--json` flag (it is reserved by pnpm). Pass a doc via `--file`, or use inline `--json` only via `npx tsx packages/cli/src/main.ts …`.
6. **If a write can't connect,** the DB is likely in its overnight sleep window (or a slow cold-start) — re-run `pnpm db:up` (works any time inside the window) and retry.
7. **Keep sessions off the same artifact.** Different artifacts never contend (per-id rows, transactional upserts); the **same** artifact across two sessions is not coordinated — claims are DBOS-deferred — so don't co-edit one artifact in parallel.
surfaces:
- **Library** — the only surface written. Each CLI write lands in the live Cloud SQL store as one event plus a transactional projection upsert; this store is the **live source of truth** for artifact state. Reads touch it only with `--pg`; offline reads come from the in-memory seed.
- **Repo** — read-only here. `apps/studio/data/knowledge.json` is the migration seed/export, and `apps/studio/data/assets.json` is **generated** from it (`build-corpus.mjs`). The ceremony writes the seed only in the dual-edit case (an existing non-agent artifact, step 4) and never the generated `assets.json`; a live-only edit leaves the seed lagging until `export-corpus --pg` (ADR-0120) or the dual edit reconciles it.
failureModes:
- **Hand-editing `knowledge.json` for a live change** — the edit is invisible to the live store, to parallel sessions, and to the studio; seed and DB silently diverge, and the next migration decides which version survives.
- **Running `load-corpus.ts --force` against a live DB carrying CLI edits** — the seed overwrites the live state and **reverts those edits**; recovery is the DB→seed export (`storytree library export-corpus --pg [--write]`, ADR-0120) — but `--write` is all-or-nothing, so a scoped recovery amid unrelated drift means a surgical seed hand-edit.
- **One-sided edit of an existing non-agent artifact** — editing only live leaves the seed (and every offline read) stale; editing only the seed is invisible to the live store and the studio. `sync-corpus` will not carry an update across (migrate-only), and `check:corpus-content` WARNs without blocking, so the drift persists until someone reconciles by hand.
- **Hand-editing the generated `assets.json`** — the next `build-corpus.mjs` run erases the edit.
- **Skipping the search step** — duplicate artifacts split authority and sever the evidence chain (asset:edit-first-curation); consumers no longer know which doc is canonical.
- **Co-editing the same artifact from two sessions** — writes are uncoordinated, so the last upsert silently wins and the other session's edit vanishes without an error.
- **Forgetting `pnpm db:up`** — writes fail to connect after an idle-stop; harmless, but the session stalls until it re-runs `db:up`.
verification: The write-path guards are machine-checked: the CLI **refuses** any write without `--pg`, `artifact new` refuses an existing id, and `validateLibraryDoc` rejects an invalid doc at the boundary — none of these can be skipped by forgetting them. Nothing, however, checks that a session searched before writing, that `knowledge.json` or the generated files weren't hand-edited, or that `load-corpus.ts --force` isn't run over live CLI edits — those steps rest entirely on the session honouring the ceremony.
provenance: Derived (per ADR-0034) from ADR-0023 §10–11 and the CLAUDE.md library section; on any disagreement the cited ADRs win. The step-4 dual seed+live edit rule was graduated 2026-07-02 from agent-memory `artifact-authoring-traps` (ADR-0095); the idle-stop and no-DB→seed-export claims were corrected on the same pass against ADR-0114 (fixed nightly sleep window) and ADR-0120 (export-corpus).

title: library
description: The shared knowledge tier — schema-validated artifact docs in the live Cloud SQL store, read just-in-time by sessions via the CLI, by humans via the studio.
oneLine: The shared **knowledge tier**: schema-validated artifact docs — reference (definitions, techstack), behavioural rules (principles, guardrails, patterns), ways-of-working (processes), roles (agents), initiative overlays (arcs, and their ephemeral plans), and transient signal units (open questions, proposals, friction) — living in the shared Cloud SQL Postgres store, read just-in-time by sessions through the CLI and by humans through the studio.
whatItIs: One of the project's three working surfaces — the story tree is the **work**, the noticeboard is the **sessions**, the library is the **knowledge** (ADR-0034). It is a corpus tier parallel to the work hierarchy: structured documents discriminated by `kind` — twelve kinds as of ADR-0183: definition, principle, pattern, guardrail, techstack, process, open-question, agent, proposal, friction (the employees' upward friction channel, ADR-0168 — a transient-lifecycle kind like open-question and proposal), arc (the initiative overlay upstream of stories and ADRs, ADR-0183), and plan (the first **ephemeral** kind — Postgres-only, excluded from `knowledge.json` and every seed ceremony, ADR-0183). The authoritative per-kind field tables are `KIND_SPECS` in `packages/library/src/knowledge.ts` — on any future mismatch, trust that table over a hand-list. Stored as **JSONB docs, zod-validated at the write boundary**, with relationships as ID references inside the docs (ADR-0017). History = events, current state = projection. The **live shared Cloud SQL Postgres store is the source of truth for artifact state** (ADR-0023 §11); git holds the code plus the *generated* `assets.json` view, and `apps/studio/data/knowledge.json` is the migration seed/export of the durable kinds — ephemeral kinds like `plan` never seed — not the edit-here surface. Sessions reach it just-in-time through the exploratory `storytree library` CLI — dashboard → kind → artifact → local DAG, choose-your-own-adventure, never a corpus dump (ADR-0023) — and humans through the studio. Artifacts are **downstream and derived**: they cite their source ADRs via `references` and reference-don't-restate; on disagreement the cited ADR wins.
whatItIsNot: Not the **studio** — the UI window onto the library (and the rest of the system), not the tier itself. Not **`docs/decisions/` ADRs** — those are the source/history layer where decisions are made; library artifacts are derived from them and cite them. Not the **`stories/` tree** — that is the work to be built; the library is the knowledge consumed while building it.
provenance: Tier returned by ADR-0017 (resolving open-q §9); named "library" by ADR-0019; live-store-as-source-of-truth and the CLI interface by ADR-0023; the three-surfaces framing and the `process` kind by ADR-0034; the tenth kind (`friction`) by ADR-0168; the `arc` initiative overlay and the first ephemeral kind (`plan`) by ADR-0183.

steps:
1. **Sync** (only when resuming an existing branch): `pnpm sync` rebases onto `origin/main`; after a rebase, push with `--force-with-lease` (fine for single-author branches — ADR-0022).
2. **Gate locally**: `pnpm gate` (repo manifest + typecheck + offline tests). It mirrors CI's `verify` job, which additionally runs the build. Red means the unit is not done — do not proceed to a non-draft PR.
3. **Commit** the unit on the session branch, never directly on `main`.
4. **Push** the branch to `origin`.
5. **Open a non-draft PR** into `main` with `gh pr create`. A `claude/real/*` promotion branch's tip is the verdict's exact attested commit — never rebase, squash, or amend it (ADR-0031).
6. **Stop.** Never run `gh pr merge` in any flavor (including `--auto`): the repo has no required checks or branch protection, so a manual merge lands *instantly, pre-CI*. The workflow's `automerge` job (`needs: verify`) merges with `--merge --delete-branch` — a true merge commit, which is what keeps a promoted verdict's commit an ancestor of `main`.
7. **To hold a PR** — the only opt-out — mark it a draft or add the `hold` label. A hold is **temporary**: when the held unit is green and ready, flip the draft to ready (`gh pr ready`) or drop the `hold` label so it merges — a finished, green unit must never stay parked in draft. Every other green non-draft PR merges. Merge to `main` quickly and frequently; the cadence is trunk-based.
8. **After the merge — the branch is dead (ADR-0142).** CI's merge job machine-clears the merged branch's board state (presence retire + story-claim release, ADR-0138), and the merged-branch guard refuses any future PR from the same head branch. To keep working: `storytree branch next --pg` — one verb: it detects the dead branch, cuts + switches a fresh `claude/<name>` from `origin/main` (the worktree and session identity survive — only the branch name dies), and re-declares presence, re-taking the story claim so the wisp re-lights on the fresh branch (offline it prints the `storytree noticeboard declare --working-on <next unit> --node <story> --pg` to run yourself). Manual fallback: `git fetch origin && git checkout -b claude/<fresh-name> origin/main`, then re-declare. Across a landing the wisp blinks; it must never silently die.
title: Merge ceremony
trigger: A unit of work is complete and the local gate is green — `pnpm gate` passes on the session's branch — with nothing red or WIP in the diff. Run the ceremony **without waiting to be asked**: sitting on green work is the failure this artifact exists to stop (ADR-0034). It also begins when picking a branch back up on another machine — start at the sync step.
surfaces:
**Repo/CI only.** Runs `pnpm gate` locally as the pre-merge check (mirrored by CI's `verify` job), then reads the working tree and branch state; writes a commit, a pushed branch, and a PR; CI's `verify` job re-runs the gate on the merge result and the `automerge` job writes the merge to git `main` and deletes the branch.

**Two trunks — do not conflate them.** This ceremony governs the **dev repo's git `main`**, the meta-layer that builds storytree. The **product story-trunk** is a different surface: it is approval-gated (ADR-0008; `trunk`, `approval-gated-trunk`) and is **never auto-merged** — a green agent result there is a request for human diff-review, not a landing. The invariant shared by both is *never merge un-green*; what differs is whether green is sufficient (here, yes — solo author) or necessary-but-not-sufficient (the product).
statement: When a unit of work is green, commit, push, and open a **non-draft PR** — then stop: the CI `automerge` job lands it on the dev repo's git `main` once `verify` passes, and a session never merges manually.
provenance: Synthesized downstream of ADR-0022/0031 per ADR-0034 (the kind's first instance); on any disagreement the cited ADR wins.
description: The dev-repo landing ceremony: when a unit of work is green, commit, push, open a non-draft PR, and stop — CI auto-merges on green; sessions never merge manually. Read before landing anything on git `main`.
failureModes:
- **Manual `gh pr merge`** merges instantly, pre-CI — no required checks exist, so the gate is bypassed entirely. This is the recorded incident: sessions reached for `gh pr merge` (including `--auto`, which on this unprotected repo also merges immediately) and landed unverified work (ADR-0034). Let the workflow merge.
- **Squash-merging a `claude/real/*` promotion PR** mints a new sha and orphans the persisted verdict's anchor; the attested commit must remain an ancestor of `main` (ADR-0031).
- **Sitting on green work**: the owner repeatedly had to tell sessions to commit and open a PR (ADR-0034's observable symptom), and unlanded work drifts across the owner's machines.
- **Red/WIP work in a non-draft PR**: the moment it happens to go green it auto-merges, and half-finished work lands on `main`. Finish the unit or mark the PR draft.
- **Leaving a finished unit in a draft hold**: marking a PR draft to pause mid-build is correct; forgetting to flip it back to ready once the unit is green re-creates 'sitting on green work' by another name — the held PR never auto-merges and the owner has to ask why it is still a draft (the recorded 2026-06-14 slip). A hold is temporary.
- **Conflating the two trunks**: waiting for a human to merge a dev PR (a stall — none is coming), or carrying auto-merge-on-green thinking onto the product story-trunk (exactly the v1 scar `approval-gated-trunk` exists to prevent).
- **Continuing on the merged branch**: the merge just machine-cleared that branch's presence and story claims, so the session keeps working *invisibly* — no wisp, no board row (the recorded 2026-07-02 incident: an active session vanished from the map after its PR merged) — and its next PR is refused by the merged-branch guard anyway. Cut a fresh branch and re-declare (ADR-0142).
verification: The green-before-merge half is deterministic: `automerge` is an Actions job in `.github/workflows/ci.yml` with `needs: verify`, gated to non-draft PRs without the `hold` label, and the `pull_request` checkout tests the merge result — nothing merges *through the workflow* un-green. The branch-dies-on-merge half is also deterministic: the merged-branch guard (`scripts/merged-branch-guard.sh`, a `verify` step) refuses a PR whose head branch already has a merged PR (ADR-0142). **Nothing checks the rest**: there is no branch protection, so no machine stops a manual `gh pr merge` — merge-only-via-the-workflow is enforced by convention (ADR-0022 says so explicitly); and non-squash for `claude/real/*` is a recorded operating rule whose enforcement automation is later work (ADR-0031).

title: Attempt privileged actions, approve inline
description: When an action needs the owner's authorization, attempt it yourself and let approval happen inline (issue the tool call so the harness surfaces its permission prompt, or ask a quick inline yes/no) — don't decline in prose and hand the command back for the owner to run in a terminal. The human still owns the sign-off; this changes the MECHANISM, not who decides.
statement: For an action that needs owner authorization — `terraform apply`, a deploy, an infra or `gh` mutation, any outward-facing or hard-to-reverse op — ATTEMPT it yourself and let approval happen INLINE (issue the tool call so the harness surfaces its permission prompt, or ask one short inline yes/no), rather than declining in prose and handing the command back for the owner to run manually; the human still owns the sign-off, so this is an approval-MECHANISM way-of-working, NOT a request to auto-allow.
trigger: A unit reaches a step that needs the owner's go-ahead before it can proceed — an irreversible, outward-facing, or otherwise privileged action (terraform/deploy/infra/`gh`/anything that touches a live external surface). The decision to do it is still the owner's (`owner-fork-bar`, `human-owns-the-outer-loop`); what this process governs is HOW you put that decision to them.
steps:
1. **Don't punt it to a terminal.** Refusing in prose — 'you should now run `terraform apply`' — and handing the command back is the failure this exists to stop: the owner is a solo operator, and context-switching to a terminal to run a command you could run is friction they asked to be gone (stated 2026-06-22, after an idle-stop `terraform apply` was punted to them).
2. **For irreversible infra, plan first.** Default to a read-only preview — `terraform plan`, a dry-run, a diff — and show its output, so the owner approves against a concrete change, not a blind apply.
3. **Issue the action and let the prompt surface.** Run the tool call; the harness's inline permission prompt IS the approval surface. Where a permission prompt doesn't apply, ask one short inline yes/no (a quick AskUserQuestion) — approving stays a single inline step, never a task handed off.
4. **Keep the decision with the owner.** Surface the decision every time — the human owns the outer-loop sign-off (`asset:human-owns-the-outer-loop`); you are making the approval one click, not removing it.
surfaces: **The session's own action surface** — the privileged tool call (terraform/deploy/`gh`/infra REST) and the harness permission prompt it raises, or an inline AskUserQuestion. No Library or repo state is written by this process itself; it shapes how an already-owner-owned action is put to the owner.
failureModes:
- **Declining in prose and handing the command back** — the recorded slip (the idle-stop `terraform apply` punted 2026-06-22): adds the exact terminal context-switch the owner wanted removed.
- **Reading it as auto-allow** — graduating 'attempt it inline' into blanket `allow`-rules in `settings.json` on the strength of this alone. This is an approval-MECHANISM preference; the owner still approves each one. Don't widen permissions off it.
- **Skipping the plan on irreversible infra** — issuing a blind `apply`/deploy without a `plan`/dry-run/diff first, so the owner approves a change they can't see.
- **Removing the human from the call** — treating 'make approval one step' as license to decide an owner-owned, irreversible, or outward-facing action yourself (the `human-owns-the-outer-loop` boundary).
verification: Not machine-checked — this is a way-of-working, not a gated path. The boundary it must not cross IS enforced elsewhere: `settings.json` permissions decide what runs without a prompt, and the inline permission prompt itself is the proof the owner approved a given action. Honouring 'attempt + inline approve' over 'decline in prose', and 'plan before irreversible apply', rests on the session following this process.
provenance: Graduated 2026-06-27 from agent-memory (ADR-0095 — feedback `attempt-privileged-actions-ask-inline`, owner preference stated 2026-06-22). A way-of-working (an approval MECHANISM), so a `process` per ADR-0034 — no existing artifact covered the mechanism (the corpus had WHO approves, not HOW the action is put to them). Cites `human-owns-the-outer-loop` (the sign-off stays the owner's; this is not auto-allow) and `owner-fork-bar` (what rises to the owner is unchanged). Consumers: session-orchestrator.

title: Stage the attestation experience
description: At an operator-attested gate (ADR-0070 stage 2 — the appearance / feel / live-SDK / spend legs a human must witness), the orchestrator STANDS UP the experience itself, VERIFIES it is actually serving, and hands the owner a confirmed-working URL/entry point plus the minimal what-to-walk — never a raw command for the owner to run and debug. Making the human's attestation frictionless is part of the orchestrator's job at that gate; the human still renders the verdict.
statement: When a unit reaches an operator-attested leg — the appearance / feel / live-SDK / spend judgment a human must witness (ADR-0070 stage 2) — the orchestrator STARTS the experience itself, VERIFIES it is genuinely serving/reachable (an HTTP probe, a marker check, or a re-run witness — never an assumption), and hands the owner a CONFIRMED-working URL plus the minimal what-to-walk / what-to-judge; a raw command is a fallback only, never the handoff. The human still renders the verdict — this makes the attestation frictionless, it does not move the sign-off.
trigger: A unit reaches a gate the leaf and the spine structurally cannot close — an `operator-attested` leg (ADR-0070 stage 2 / ADR-0007): the appearance of a visual surface, the feel of an interaction, a live-SDK or spend witness — where a human owner must observe a running experience and sign the verdict. This is the moment the orchestrator would otherwise chip the attestation item and hand the owner a command; it governs HOW that gate is put to the owner.
steps:
1. **Stand it up yourself — don't punt a command.** Start the preview / experience the owner must witness (`pnpm --filter studio dev` or `studio:up`; a hosted deep-link; the built site served the way the owner will actually view it), from the right directory with the right runner. Handing the owner a bare shell line to run is the failure this exists to stop — an environment-specific break (wrong cwd, a local dep like `astro` not on PATH so it needs `npx`, a missing `pnpm install`) stalls the attestation and pushes avoidable debugging onto the owner before they can even begin (the recorded slip: the increment-H UAT preview, 2026-07-04).
2. **Verify it is actually serving — probe, don't assume.** Confirm reachability with a real signal before you hand it over: an HTTP probe (`curl -sSf <url>` / a fetch to the health or entry route returning 2xx), a marker/content check that the expected surface rendered, or a re-run witness — never 'I started it, so it's up' (the same probe-don't-assume discipline as `asset:verify-edit-write-persisted-or-escalate` and the DB probe rule). If it will not serve, that is the orchestrator's to resolve or escalate — not the owner's to discover.
3. **Hand over a confirmed URL + the minimal walk.** Give the owner the working URL/entry point you just confirmed, plus a short 'what to walk / what to judge' (the deep-link to open, the interaction to try, the specific look/feel to rule on). Keep any raw command only as a fallback beneath the confirmed link, not as the primary handoff.
4. **Keep the verdict with the owner — do not cross into signing it.** Standing up and confirming the experience is the whole of the orchestrator's job here; the `operator-attested` verdict is the owner's and only the owner's (`asset:agent-never-self-exempts`, `asset:human-owns-the-outer-loop`). You remove the friction, not the judgment — never self-sign the look/feel because you made it easy to witness.
surfaces: **The session's own action surface** — the preview/experience the orchestrator starts (studio dev/`studio:up`, a served build, a hosted deep-link) and the probe that confirms it serves; the handoff message carrying the confirmed URL + the what-to-walk. **Repo/CI** — none written by this process; it composes with the merge/prove-and-promote ceremonies, it does not replace them. No Library or story-tree state is written — the `operator-attested` verdict is recorded through the existing promotion/attestation path once the owner signs, not by this process.
failureModes:
- **Punting a command instead of a running experience** — the recorded slip (the increment-H UAT gate, 2026-07-04): the orchestrator chipped the attestation and handed over a shell command to serve the preview; it failed in the owner's environment (wrong directory; `astro` is a local `web/` dependency not on PATH, needing `npx`), so the owner had to debug it before testing. The owner's direction: 'if you want uat human testing then you should do what you need to to have this up and running so i can easily do the testing.' A gate presented as 'here's a command, go run it' is a defect.
- **Assuming it serves without probing** — declaring the experience ready off 'I ran the start command' when the server never bound, the build 404s, or the tab is occluded (`preview_screenshot` hangs on a hidden tab). Confirm with a real probe/marker/witness, the way only the spine's observation counts for red/green, not the leaf's say-so.
- **Crossing from staging into signing** — treating 'I made the attestation frictionless' as license to record the `operator-attested` verdict yourself. The whole point of stage 2 (ADR-0070) is the one judgment the agent structurally cannot make; making it easy to witness never makes it yours to sign (`agent-never-self-exempts`).
- **Over-reading it as auto-serve for everything** — this is the operator-attested handoff, not a mandate to spin up servers for every message; it fires at the gate where a human must witness a running experience.
verification: Not machine-checked — this is a way-of-working (an attestation-handoff MECHANISM), not a gated path, so a `process` per ADR-0034. The boundary it must not cross IS enforced elsewhere: `operator-attested` can never be self-granted by an agent (ADR-0007 / the proof model / `asset:agent-never-self-exempts`), so nothing this process does can mint the verdict — it only removes the friction before the owner's sign-off. Honouring 'stand it up + probe + hand over a confirmed URL' over 'punt a command' rests on the session following this process; the probe in step 2 is the session's own honesty check that the experience is genuinely reachable.
provenance: Graduated 2026-07-04 from agent-memory at the owner's direction — after the increment-H website UAT gate, where the orchestrator chipped the operator-attested item and handed the owner a raw shell command to serve the preview; the command failed in the owner's environment (wrong cwd; `astro` a local `web/` dep needing `npx`), so the owner had to debug it before they could test, and directed the guidance be updated ('if you want uat human testing then you should do what you need to to have this up and running so i can easily do the testing'). A way-of-working (an approval/attestation MECHANISM), so a `process` per ADR-0034 — the sibling of `attempt-privileged-actions-approve-inline` (that one: don't punt a privileged COMMAND to the owner's terminal; this one: don't punt an operator-attested EXPERIENCE), and the outer-loop counterpart of `frontend-builder`'s inner-loop 'surface a hosted deep-link and STOP' (ADR-0070 §3) — the orchestrator RUNNING the gate must stand the experience up and confirm it serves, not relay a leaf's deep-link untested. Grounded in ADR-0070 (the two-stage proof; operator-attested is stage 2) and ADR-0007 (operator-attested is never self-granted). Consumers: session-orchestrator (composes with `frontend-builder`).

title: noticeboard
description: The session-presence surface: parallel sessions declare who exists, in which worktree, working on what — advisory-only, staleness-aged, never blocking (ADR-0033).
oneLine: The session-presence surface — one of the three working surfaces (tree = the work, **noticeboard** = the sessions, library = the knowledge): parallel sessions declare "I exist, in this worktree, working on X" anchored to story nodes; advisory-only, staleness-aged, never blocking.
whatItIs: The **coordination organ** among the three working surfaces: the tree shows the work, the library holds the knowledge, the noticeboard shows the **sessions**. Each parallel session upserts one declaration — `workingOn` prose plus optional work-hierarchy node ids — over an append-only history (`events.session_event` + `events.session`, the house event+projection pattern). **Identity is the worktree name** — derived, never typed — and there is **no signer chain**: presence is not proof, so ADR-0020's fail-closed signing posture deliberately does not apply. **Staleness replaces release discipline**: the board ages rows by `lastSeenAt`; `status: done` is politeness, not a requirement. Automation declares presence only through never-blocking paths — plain spine code around builds, fire-and-forget session hooks, a statusline glance — **never a blocking-capable hook**, and a board write failure never fails the enclosing action. Presence is live-DB only (`pnpm db:up`); offline surfaces degrade gracefully, rendering without presence lines rather than failing. Surfaces: `storytree noticeboard` (the dedicated board + `declare`/`done`) and the presence block woven into `storytree tree` (ADR-0023's choose-your-own-adventure pattern, extended). Since ADR-0142, a deliberate `declare --node` ALSO takes the story's work-time claim (`events.node_claim`, ADR-0138) — one ceremony step = presence + wisp — and `done` releases the session's claims; the claim write is fail-soft, so a claim refusal (surfaced, naming the holder) never fails the presence declare. Defined by ADR-0033; built through the prove-it-gate as `stories/notice-board` — per-capability status lives in the story, not here.
whatItIsNot: Not the **feedback organ** — cites, archival and synthesis belong to `stories/feedback-graduation` (ADR-0032); the noticeboard carries presence only. Not itself a **lock** — presence stays advisory and a declare never fails on a conflict. The typed claims-with-refusal upgrade ADR-0033 named-deferred WAS built once overlap became routine (`events.node_claim`: builds ADR-0121, the coordination layer ADR-0138, acquisition at `declare --node` ADR-0142) — but that claim layer is a separate primitive the declare *drives*, not the presence rows themselves: a refusal names the holder and is surfaced loudly, never blocking the presence write. Not a **proof surface** — declarations carry no signer chain and attest nothing; verdicts and the gate's signing posture (ADR-0020) live elsewhere.
