It also absorbs ADR-0032 §3's original memory-graduation synthesis seat — the same judgment applied to the agent-memory source (the librarian-curator runs the routine pre-merge memory pass, the synthesist is the deeper whole-system adjudicator both paths feed). Until this build the librarian-curator held the chair under the same role-agnostic ceremony (ADR-0168 D5); it now runs only the bounded routine pre-merge drain and hands the deeper adjudication here.

title: Rename: 'UAT tests' → 'UAT test criteria' (de-overload 'tests')
description: A deferred vocabulary rename: the story-level acceptance items (ADR-0044's per-test 'UAT tests' under `## Story UAT`) become **UAT test criteria**, so 'tests' stops colliding with `node:test` unit tests. node:test, contracts, and the stored `#uat-<n>` ids stay unchanged. Parked until a quiet, no-active-sessions window.
summary: **Staleness note (2026-07-06):** authored against the pre-dissolution layout — `packages/core/src/uat-tests.ts` is now `packages/library/src/uat-tests.ts` (ADR-0068), and `docs/glossary.md` has since been RETIRED (ADR-0135; drop that sweep item). Re-verify the touched-surfaces map at kickoff. — Rename the story-level acceptance items — ADR-0044's per-test 'UAT tests', parsed from each story's `## Story UAT` section into `<story>#uat-<n>` — to **UAT test criteria** (singular: a **UAT test criterion**), so the word **tests** stops colliding with `node:test` unit tests. The decision is made; only the execution is deferred to a quiet window.
motivation: The single word *tests* is overloaded across storytree, and a reader can't always tell which sense is meant. It names at least four different things: (a) `node:test` UNIT tests in `*.test.ts` — the real, runnable, automated suites (`pnpm -r test`); (b) per-capability **contracts**, each defined as 'one automated, isolated unit test', the leaf of the proof ladder; (c) the story-level **acceptance walkthrough** items under `## Story UAT`, which ADR-0044 calls 'UAT tests' (`<story>#uat-<n>`, the attestation surface); and (d) the informal 'blind test / dogfood test'. The sharpest collision is in the studio, which labels the acceptance list **'UAT tests'** in a panel sitting right beside the runnable suites — and in prose where 'the UAT tests' could mean either a thing you *run* or a thing you *satisfy*. Naming the story-level acceptance items **UAT test criteria** disambiguates the one tier that most invites confusion: a *criterion* is a condition to be satisfied or attested, not a suite to be executed — which is exactly what an ADR-0044 acceptance item is (it is attested by a human or a machine, never 'run' as a `node:test`). The genuinely-runnable senses (a, b) keep the word *test*. The cost of leaving it: every new story author re-meets the 'which test is this?' ambiguity, and the UI keeps mixing acceptance criteria with executable suites.
change:
Before → after (name the old and new term exactly):

**Vocabulary (the heart of the rename):**
- One acceptance item — today **a UAT test** (ADR-0044) → **a UAT test criterion**.
- The list of them — today **UAT tests** → **UAT test criteria**.
- The framing stays a *walkthrough*: the `## Story UAT` section is still the story's user-acceptance walkthrough (the `uat` library definition — 'user-acceptance walkthrough' — is UNCHANGED); only the per-item noun moves from 'UAT test' to 'UAT test criterion'.

**Section heading (a SUB-DECISION — confirm the exact wording with the owner at execution, like the precedent's tree-vs-capability grain):**
- RECOMMENDED: `## Story UAT` → `## UAT Test Criteria`, for consistency with the new noun. This is the riskiest single change because the heading is the parser anchor `STORY_UAT_HEADING` (`packages/core/src/uat-tests.ts`) — it MUST move in lockstep with that regex, transitionally accepting BOTH headings so no story's criteria parse to `[]` mid-migration.
- ALTERNATIVE: keep `## Story UAT` and change only the item vocabulary + identifiers — a clean partial if the heading question can't be settled.

**Code identifiers (`packages/core` + consumers):**
- module `packages/core/src/uat-tests.ts` → `uat-test-criteria.ts`
- type `UatTest` → `UatTestCriterion`
- field `uatTests` (on `NodeSpec`) → `uatTestCriteria`
- fn `parseUatTests(...)` → `parseUatTestCriteria(...)`
- fn `uatTestId(...)` → `uatTestCriterionId(...)` (NB: its OUTPUT string scheme `<story>#uat-<n>` does NOT change — see Scope)
- OPTIONAL/low-value: the per-criterion witness enum `UatTestWitness` / `UAT_TEST_WITNESSES` → `*Criterion*` for consistency; can be deferred (it names a *witness*, not a 'test').

**User-facing labels:**
- studio panel heading `UAT tests ({n})` (`apps/studio/src/components/TreeView.tsx`) → `UAT test criteria ({n})`; CSS `.uat-tests` / `.uat-test-*` and the `uatTestsForStory` / `/api/attestations` field names → `uatTestCriteria*`.
- `storytree tree`'s `UAT tests:` block label (`packages/cli/src/tree.ts`) → `UAT test criteria:`.

**JUDGEMENT CALL — surfaced, not guessed (per the brief):** the per-capability **contracts** (b) STAY 'contracts'. This is a category decision, not a style one: a contract is defined as 'one automated, isolated unit test', and ADR-0010 places UAT at the STORY level only (capabilities get integration tests, contracts get unit tests, NOT UATs). Calling a contract a 'UAT test criterion' would be a category error, so contracts keep their already-distinct, well-chosen name. Likewise `node:test` unit tests (a) stay 'tests' (they are genuine tests) and the informal 'blind/dogfood test' (d) stays (colloquial, not a structured term).
scope:
**Surfaces / files / identifiers touched:**
- `packages/core`: `src/uat-tests.ts` (the `UatTest` schema, `parseUatTests`, `uatTestId`, and the `STORY_UAT_HEADING` regex), `src/uat-tests.test.ts`, and the `@storytree/core` barrel `src/index.ts` re-exports.
- `packages/orchestrator`: `src/node-spec.ts` (imports `parseUatTests` + `UatTest`; the `NodeSpec.uatTests` field at `:82`,`:129`); `src/story-build.test.ts` fixtures (`uatTests: []`).
- `packages/cli`: `src/tree.ts` (the `UAT tests:` render block + `UatTest` import at `:13`,`:161`,`:237-241`); `src/attest.ts` (help/next prose says 'per-UAT-test'); fixtures in `src/oq-gate.test.ts`, `src/gate-as-proof.test.ts`.
- `apps/studio`: `src/components/TreeView.tsx` ('UAT tests' panel heading + `.uat-test-*`), `src/types.ts` (the `UatTest` type + GET `/api/attestations` response shape), `src/index.css` (`.uat-tests` / `.uat-test-cell` / `.uat-test-title` / `.uat-test-who`), `src/api.ts`, `server/apiRouter.ts` (`uatTestsForStory`, the `/api/attestations` endpoint), `server/libraryBackend.ts`.
- `stories/**`: the `## Story UAT` heading + 'UAT test' prose in the 11 story files that carry the section (uat-attestation, studio, studio-members ×3, studio-cloud, notice-board, library, feedback-graduation, drive-machinery, ci-cd). NOTE: `stories/drive-machinery/story.md` was just extended (2026-06-15) with Story UAT criterion 7 in the CURRENT vocabulary — a prime example of exactly the prose this targets.
- `docs/`: ADR-0044 prose (and its ADR-0040 cross-refs), `docs/glossary.md` if it carries the term, and the `uat` library-definition body where it says 'UAT tests'.
- Library: the `uat` definition artifact (live store + `knowledge.json` seed) and this proposal artifact.

**STORED DATA — the riskiest part, explicitly a NON-GOAL:** the attestation join key `<story>#uat-<n>` (the output of `uatTestId`) and the `events.attestation.test_id` column are STORED, append-only, SIGNED ids. Renaming the scheme or the column would ORPHAN every recorded attestation (prod already carries a synthetic `_deploy-verify#uat-1` row; ADR-0044) and force a migration of a signed log. LEAVE BOTH UNCHANGED — they are opaque stable keys; the 'uat' in them does not collide with 'test', and renaming them buys nothing against real data-loss risk. This is the single most important non-goal.

**Other explicit NON-GOALS (the word 'test' stays):** `node:test` unit tests, `*.test.ts`, `pnpm -r test`, the gate (a — genuine tests); the per-capability **contracts** (b — a category error to rename, see The change); ADR-0040's story-level witness vocabulary (`uat_witness` frontmatter, `UatWitness`, `effectiveUatWitness` — it names who *witnessed*, not the criteria list); the npm scope `@storytree/*`, the `storytree` CLI binary, and the DB schema beyond the `test_id` column comment.
migration:
Run as ONE dedicated session, core-first, gating green at each step:

1. **Record the decision.** Allocate + land an ADR (`pnpm storytree adr new --title "..." --pg`) capturing the rename, the contracts-stay rationale (ADR-0010 category argument), and the stored-id non-goal — so the rename has a deciding record.
2. **Pin the heading wording with the owner** (`## Story UAT` → `## UAT Test Criteria`, or keep `## Story UAT`). This is the one term that must be settled before any story file is touched.
3. **`packages/core` first.** Rename `uat-tests.ts` → `uat-test-criteria.ts`; `UatTest`→`UatTestCriterion`, `uatTests`→`uatTestCriteria`, `parseUatTests`→`parseUatTestCriteria`, `uatTestId`→`uatTestCriterionId`. If the heading changes, WIDEN `STORY_UAT_HEADING` to a transitional alternation accepting BOTH old and new headings (so nothing parses to `[]` mid-flight). Update `src/index.ts` re-exports + the test file. Verify: `pnpm --filter @storytree/core test` green.
4. **Sweep consumers in one pass.** `packages/orchestrator/src/node-spec.ts`, `packages/cli/src/tree.ts` + `attest.ts`, and all fixtures (`uatTests: []` → `uatTestCriteria: []`). Verify: `pnpm -r typecheck && pnpm -r test` green. (Watch the pinned id-list tests — update them in the same commit, per the real-build-typecheck-wall lesson.)
5. **Studio.** Rename the panel heading + `.uat-test-*` classes + `uatTestsForStory` + the `/api/attestations` field names + `src/types.ts`. Verify: `pnpm --filter studio build` + `pnpm --filter studio test` green.
6. **Story files.** Rename the `## Story UAT` headings (if step 2 said so) + 'UAT test' prose across the 11 story files; re-run `parseUatTestCriteria` per story (e.g. `storytree tree <story>`) to confirm the SAME criteria still parse with UNCHANGED ids. Then, if the regex was widened in step 3, tighten it to the new heading only.
7. **Library.** `storytree library artifact edit uat --set ... --pg` to fix 'UAT tests' → 'UAT test criteria' in the `uat` definition body; migrate the `knowledge.json` seed + regenerate the derived view (`npx tsx apps/studio/data/build-corpus.mjs`); retire or keep this proposal.
8. **Prose sweep.** ADR-0044, CLAUDE.md, README, docs for 'UAT test(s)' → 'UAT test criterion/criteria' at the acceptance grain.
9. **Full gate + smoke.** `pnpm gate` green; studio smoke (the 'UAT test criteria' panel renders, attestation ids unchanged).
readiness: Run in a QUIET window: `storytree noticeboard` shows no other active sessions, and NO open PR touches `packages/core`, `apps/studio`, or `stories/**` — a sweep this wide conflicts with any parallel branch, and story files are actively edited (criterion 7 just landed on drive-machinery). Kick off only from a green `pnpm gate` on `main`. Bring the DB up for the `uat`-definition edit (step 7) but otherwise leave it idle. The owner must have confirmed the `## Story UAT` heading wording (step 2) first. Because it spans core + orchestrator + cli + studio + stories + docs + the library in one coherent sweep, give it ONE dedicated session, not interleaved with feature work. A session knows it is time when: no conflicting PRs are open, the gate is green on `main`, and the heading wording is settled.
risks:
**The parser is the sharp edge.** If a `## Story UAT` heading is renamed but `STORY_UAT_HEADING` is not widened in lockstep, `parseUatTestCriteria` silently returns `[]` — a story's criteria AND their whole attestation surface vanish with no error. Mitigation: do `packages/core` first with a transitional dual-accept regex; verify every migrated story re-parses to the SAME criterion ids before tightening it.
**Never touch the stored ids.** Renaming `<story>#uat-<n>` or `events.attestation.test_id` orphans signed attestations (the data-loss path) — keep both verbatim; the rename is vocabulary + identifiers + labels only.
**Pinned-test churn.** Id-list / fixture tests break when identifiers move — update them in the same commit (real-build-typecheck-wall lesson).
**Cosmetic residue.** A missed 'UAT test' string in prose is harmless, not load-bearing.
**Fallback if blocked:** if the heading question can't be settled, ship identifier + prose + label renames only and leave the heading as `## Story UAT` — a clean partial with no parser risk.

title: One element per signal
description: The forest world is the observability layer rendered: each observed signal gets its own distinct art element, and an island's complexity emerges from the stack — never from a single "complexity" score.
statement: In the `#/tree` forest world, every observed signal renders as its OWN distinct art element; an island's complexity EMERGES from the stack of those elements, never from a single "complexity" score.
why: This is the rendering-side counterpart to `observability-first` (the data side: every state change is a typed event). Prettiness is not meaning — a substrate made to *look* intricate carries no fact, and a single complexity number is the wrong shape because there is no one value an island's form should map to. An island is intricate because the story it represents genuinely has more going on, and each thing it has is drawn by its own element. Without this rule, every new element re-litigates "what does the look mean?", elements get overloaded with two facts at once, and the world drifts from a faithful read of observed signals into decorative noise the studio can no longer be trusted to render.
howToApply: Keep the encoding channels **orthogonal**: LOCATION is the DAG's job (rank, dependencies, load-bearing centring — ADR-0036); FORM is the stack of signal→element mappings; the SUBSTRATE (the tile ground) is the deliberately signal-less canvas whose only job is to host the meaningful elements gracefully at any size. Current bindings: **capabilities → island size**, **tests → plants/flora**, **state → the central tree**, **proof verdict → hue** (ADR-0040), **presence/builds → wisps**. Run the test on every new observable fact: a new fact ⇒ add a new distinct element — do NOT overload an existing one or invent a composite "complexity" reading. Keep each signal→element a cleanly-factored renderable, but do NOT build a pluggable "world-models" abstraction until 2–3 real models actually exist (factor the seam out when it is earned, not in anticipation).

title: Retire the generated apps/studio/data/assets.json
description: Delete the committed, generated `apps/studio/data/assets.json` and re-home its consumers, so no static generated file stands in for DB-backed artifact state — anything that needs the corpus wakes the live DB or derives from the structured `knowledge.json` seed instead. Parked as a proposal (owner steer 2026-06-16: deferred behind other in-flight work); the offline-backend fork (A/B/C below) is chosen at kickoff.
summary: **Staleness note (2026-07-06):** authored against the pre-dissolution layout — `packages/core` → `packages/library` (ADR-0068), `packages/store/src/load-corpus.ts` → `packages/library/src/store/load-corpus.ts` (ADR-0077) — and `docs/glossary.md` has since been RETIRED (ADR-0135), so the 'glossary generation stays' half of the change section is moot (`build-corpus.mjs` is assets-only now); the kind table has also since grown to twelve (`friction` ADR-0168; `arc` + the ephemeral `plan` ADR-0183), so the template count is now 13 (12 generated + the bespoke `template-adr`). Re-verify the full consumer map at kickoff. — Remove the committed, generated `apps/studio/data/assets.json` — a drift-prone derivative of DB-backed artifact state — and re-home its four consumers (templates → `packages/core`; the migration; the offline studio backend; a WARN-only CLI count), honouring the principle that DB-backed data is woken from the live store, not read from a static file.
motivation: `assets.json` is a GENERATED derivative of `knowledge.json` (rendered by `apps/studio/data/build-corpus.mjs`) that duplicates artifact state the live Cloud SQL store now owns canonically (ADR-0023). Nothing gates its freshness, so it has already drifted: as of 2026-06-16 it holds 119 non-template units vs `knowledge.json`'s 124 (pattern 12 vs 15, principle 34 vs 36) — a parallel guidance-curators session edited the seed without regenerating, and nothing caught it. It is also the sole reason a latent inconsistency exists: `build-corpus.mjs` emits a `provenance` field into each asset, the live schema REJECTS `provenance`, and `load-corpus.ts` silently strips it before upsert — a round-trip that only exists because of the file. The hosted/default studio already does NOT read it (`selectedStore()` defaults to `pg` → `PgBackend`); `assets.json` only re-enters runtime through the offline JSON backend, the migration's template seed, the studio Playwright UAT, and a WARN. The cost of NOT acting is a permanently-stale committed file that three different docs already cite at three different stale counts (88 / 81 / 129), eroding trust in the seed.
change:
Verified consumer map (each confirmed against code, not the brief) and its before→after:

1. TEMPLATES (the one hard dependency). `load-corpus.ts` reads ONLY the 10 `template`-category artifacts from `assets.json`; the structured kinds already come from `knowledge.json`. 9 of the 10 (`template-definition/principle/pattern/guardrail/techstack/process/open-question/agent/proposal`) are fully regenerated from `generateTemplate(kind)` + `KIND_SPECS`, both exported from `@storytree/core`; only `template-adr` is bespoke ~1 KB content. → Re-home all 10 into a new `packages/core` module (e.g. `templates.ts`) that synthesises the 9 generated ones and holds `template-adr` as a literal. `load-corpus.ts` imports it directly instead of reading `assets.json`. This also deletes the `provenance` round-trip bug.

2. THE MIGRATION (`packages/store/src/load-corpus.ts:63`). → Drop the `assets.json` read; pull templates from the new core module; keep reading `knowledge.json` for the structured units (unchanged).

3. THE OFFLINE JSON STUDIO BACKEND (`apps/studio/server/libraryBackend.ts` `JsonBackend`, active only under `STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE=json`). This is the linchpin and the A/B/C fork — see Readiness. It is NOT merely a dev fallback: `apps/studio/uat/story-uat.spec.ts` pins the server to it (`STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE=json`) and uses `assets.json` as the live read/write store (asserting byte-equality), so it is the studio organism's deliberate DB-free UAT seam (ADR-0010 §5's mock-UAT allowance).

4. THE CLI WARN (`packages/cli/src/commands.ts:183` `generatedAssetCount`, surfaced by `storytree library --check`). → Delete it: it exists ONLY to detect `assets.json` staleness, so it is pointless once the file is gone. Drop the matching `health.ts` count-reconciliation check + its tests.

5. GLOSSARY GENERATION STAYS. `build-corpus.mjs` generates BOTH `assets.json` AND `docs/glossary.md`; the glossary path reads `knowledge.json` only, so it survives independently. → Slim `build-corpus.mjs` to glossary-only (delete the `buildAssets` half), or split it into `build-glossary.mjs`.

UNCHANGED: `knowledge.json` (the structured seed, ADR-0018), the hosted/default `PgBackend` studio, `pnpm gate`/CI (never read or hard-checked it), and `build:claude` / `storytree agents` / the generated CLAUDE.md region (read `knowledge.json` via `loadCorpus`).
scope: Files touched: `packages/core/` (new `templates.ts` + export); `packages/store/src/load-corpus.ts` (drop the assets read); `packages/cli/src/commands.ts` + `health.ts` + their tests (drop the WARN); `apps/studio/data/build-corpus.mjs` (slim to glossary-only); `apps/studio/server/libraryBackend.ts` + `serveApi.integration.test.ts` + `apps/studio/uat/story-uat.spec.ts` (per the chosen A/B/C option); the deleted `apps/studio/data/assets.json` itself; and prose sweep (`CLAUDE.md`, `docs/glossary.md` line 266, `apps/studio/README.md`, `README.md`, and the ~6 `stories/studio` + `stories/library` specs that bake `assets.json` in). Stored data: none in the live DB changes (templates are already migrated; this only changes where a FUTURE re-seed sources them). Explicit NON-goals: do NOT touch `knowledge.json` as the structured source, the live Postgres store as canonical state, the hosted studio, or the glossary's status as a generated view. Manifest-safe: `assets.json` is not listed in `repo-manifest.json` and is nested (not a root surface file), so deleting it needs no manifest edit.
migration: 1. Reserve an ADR at kickoff (`pnpm db:up` then `pnpm storytree adr new --title "Retire the generated assets.json" --pg`, ADR-0050 allocator — never hand-pick; latest on disk is 0064) recording the decision and the chosen A/B/C option. 2. Add `packages/core/templates.ts` (9 generated via `generateTemplate` + a `template-adr` literal); `pnpm --filter @storytree/core test` green. 3. Point `load-corpus.ts` at the core templates, drop the `assets.json` read; prove the migration still loads all 10 templates (offline `InMemoryStore` parity test). 4. Apply the chosen offline-backend option (A/B/C). 5. Delete the `generatedAssetCount` WARN + its tests. 6. Slim `build-corpus.mjs` to glossary-only; run it and confirm `docs/glossary.md` is byte-identical. 7. Delete `apps/studio/data/assets.json`. 8. Prose sweep. 9. `pnpm gate` green (per-package locally if the box OOMs; CI is authoritative) + a studio smoke. Route the provable pieces (templates-in-core, load-corpus parity, the backend change) through the inner loop where each has an isolatable red→green test.
readiness: Run in a quiet window (deferred 2026-06-16 behind other in-flight work). The decision still open at kickoff is the offline JSON backend's fate — pick ONE: (A) FULL RETIRE — delete `JsonBackend` + `STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE=json`; the studio always uses the live DB (db:up / ADR-0049 hosted self-wake / the Start-DB banner); re-author the studio UAT against the DB (loses the offline-runnable acceptance proof, now needs credentials) and drop the JSON cases from `serveApi.integration.test.ts`. Purest reading of the principle, highest churn. (B) RECOMMENDED — DERIVE the offline view from `knowledge.json`: delete the committed `assets.json` but keep the offline backend, deriving its corpus from `knowledge.json` (rendered via core) on read, with create/edit/delete writing to a gitignored runtime file (seeded on first run; satisfies the UAT's cold-restart durability step). Honours the principle (no committed generated derivative stands in for DB data) while preserving the offline studio + its DB-free UAT seam. (C) KEEP IT, STOP IT ROTTING — do not remove it; add a gate check that regenerates and diffs (fail on stale) and fix the `provenance` bug. Cheapest, rejects the removal ask — only if the offline UAT seam is judged worth more than the cleanup. Steps 1–2, 5–8 (templates-to-core, drop the WARN, slim build-corpus, delete the file) apply to A and B regardless; only step 4 differs by option.
risks: The studio UAT seam is the main hazard: a naive `rm assets.json` leaves the offline studio rendering an empty Library and breaks `story-uat.spec.ts` (and the `stories/studio/*` UATs that shadow it) — which is exactly why the offline-backend fork (A/B/C) must be decided, not assumed. `serveApi.integration.test.ts` exercises `JsonBackend` and IS in the offline gate (`pnpm -r test`), so option A must update it. Secondary: a half-done re-home (templates moved to core but `load-corpus` still reading the file) silently drops templates on the next re-seed — gate each step green and add an `InMemoryStore` parity test asserting all 10 templates load. Tertiary: dangling `asset:`/`doc:` references after the prose sweep — run the referential-integrity check (ADR-0026). Low-risk on the live DB: templates are already migrated, so deleting the file changes only a future re-seed's source, not current state.

title: Solar-system world: central wiring hubs (cli/store) with every connection visible
description: Reframe the #/tree observability world so the wiring layer (cli, store) sits at the centre as hub nodes and EVERY cross-package connection — including to the hubs — is visible, de-noised visually rather than hidden.
summary: Show the FULL architecture in the world: cli + store as central hub nodes that every organism connects to, with ALL edges rendered (the dense hub-spokes at low visual salience, e.g. very thin) — a radial 'solar system' layout rather than a pure tree — so badly-architected or agent-introduced coupling is visible at a glance instead of hidden.
motivation: The world's job is observability: to SEE the real architecture. The boundary-gate discussion (ADR-0074) surfaced that cli and store connect to nearly every organism. Hiding those connections (the rejected 'composition roots are exempt' idea) removes exactly the signal we most want — if an agent wires something wrongly, or a brownfield system is badly architected, that must be VISIBLE, not invisible. A tree/forest metaphor tangles when a few nodes connect to everything; a radial hub-and-spoke (solar-system) layout expresses 'these are central, everything orbits them' cleanly, and lets the dense hub-spokes be de-emphasised (thin, low-salience) instead of dropped.
change: Today: #/tree renders a forest of story-trees with dependency edges (roads, ADR-0073); the wiring layer (cli/store) is not represented as nodes at all. Proposed: cli and store become first-class central hub nodes; every organism declares its full connection surface (to other organisms AND to the hubs, owner call 2026-06-19 — one structured declaration per organism); every such edge is drawn; the layout shifts toward radial/solar-system with the hubs at the centre and organisms orbiting; the dense hub-spokes render at low visual salience so the graph stays readable while staying COMPLETE. Open fork: whether this replaces the forest metaphor wholesale or is a hub-centric LAYOUT realised within it (roads converging on a central clearing).
scope: Touches apps/studio's #/tree world (layout + edge rendering + the new hub nodes) and the data feeding it (/api/tree + the per-organism connection declarations it shares with ADR-0074's gate). Preserves the existing per-signal art mappings (ADR-0062 one-element-per-signal: plants=tests, etc.) — the LAYOUT and the hub nodes change, not the signal vocabulary. Does NOT decide the gate's enforcement model (ADR-0074) beyond rendering the same declarations. Non-goal: removing the forest's existing signals.
migration: 1. Settle the data model with ADR-0074: each organism owns one structured declaration of its cross-package connections (the gate enforces it; the world renders it). 2. Add cli/store as hub nodes in the /api/tree payload. 3. Prototype the radial/hub layout behind a flag (default off, byte-identical when off — the house pattern for world changes) and de-noise the hub-spokes. 4. Operator-attested appearance review (frontend-builder two-stage proof: geometry red-green + owner screenshot-nod). 5. Flip the default on the owner's nod.
readiness: Depends on ADR-0074's ownership/declaration model landing first (the world renders what the gate declares). Best built by the frontend-builder inner-loop agent. Forest-world is a hot surface — coordinate via the notice board. Decision still open with the owner: solar-system reskin vs hub-layout-within-the-forest.
risks: A large departure from the current forest/roads aesthetic (ADR-0073) with real investment behind it — may land better as a hub-centric layout WITHIN the forest than a wholesale solar-system reskin; settle that fork before building. Hub-and-spoke still tangles if every edge is full-salience — the de-noising (thin/low-salience spokes) is load-bearing, not optional. Keep two concerns separable: the LAYOUT change (this proposal) and the gate's declaration model (ADR-0074).

title: consuming surface
description: A node that wires organisms together to deliver an experience — an apps/* app or the public-website subrepo — and is consumed by nothing: a SINK at the top of the dependency order, a first-class node in the observability/boundary graph rather than an exempt wiring layer.
oneLine: A consuming sink at the top of the dependency order — an app or the public subrepo — that wires organisms together and is itself depended on by nothing, yet is a first-class, enforced node in the boundary graph, not an exempt wiring layer.
whatItIs: A **surface** is a CONSUMING node in the cross-story dependency graph: an `apps/*` application or the public-website subrepo that composes reusable organisms into an experience. It is a **sink** — it sits at the top of the dependency order and nothing depends on it (no inbound edge, never `foundational`). Its OUTBOUND code edges are covered, rendered, and enforced exactly like an organism's: every `@storytree/*` dependency must be a declared cross-story edge in the surface's own story `depends_on`, so the surface's true wiring is visible in the world and a miswiring is a red gate. The rule that puts a surface in the graph is **visibility-over-exemption generalised past packages**: the observability world's whole job is to show coupling, so the wiring/app layer is the signal you most want, not noise to hide (ADR-0074 §1/§2, extended to consuming surfaces by ADR-0100). A second-order property follows by construction: **bringing a surface into the scan forces its latent cross-story tangles to the surface** — the studio's hidden `studio-cloud → studio → studio-members → studio-cloud` cycle only became visible once the studio app was scanned, and was then resolved by the dependency-direction test, not tolerated (asset:cross-story-dependency). The evidence mechanism differs by surface kind: an `apps/*` app's edge is a package import the boundary scan covers; the public subrepo's edge is a synced build artifact whose liveness is proven by a drift gate (it consumes the built output, never the source), so it is a declared story node, not a scanned package.
whatItIsNot: Not an `organism` (asset:boundary): an organism is a REUSABLE bounded context other stories depend on (it draws inbound edges and may be `foundational`); a surface is consumed by no one. Not an exempt 'composition root' or 'just glue': the temptation is to hide the most-connected wiring nodes to reduce noise — rejected, because hiding them hides the most architecturally important relationships (the same call ADR-0074 §2 made for the cli/store hubs). Not a new kind of dependency EDGE: a surface's outbound edges obey the unchanged cross-story dependency-direction and no-cycle rules (asset:cross-story-dependency) — `surface` names the kind of NODE, not a different edge semantics.
provenance: Graduated (ADR-0095) from ADR-0100, which settled with the owner on 2026-06-24 that the boundary/observability graph must include consuming surfaces (apps + the public subrepo), not just organism packages. Distils the durable essence ADR-0074 §1/§2 left implicit (visibility-over-exemption stated only for packages) and ADR-0100 made explicit for surfaces: the sink-at-the-top node kind, the same-rule-as-an-organism coverage of its outbound edges, and the surfacing-a-surface-surfaces-its-latent-cycles property (the studio/studio-cloud/studio-members cycle). Consumers: story-author (classifies a surface and wires its depends_on), session-orchestrator / librarian-curator (reason about the observability world). The behavioural rule it rides on for cycle resolution is asset:cross-story-dependency.

title: The CLI surface is a superset of the UI
description: Borrow the UI's workflow vocabulary on top of an agent-facing command surface, but keep a uniform grain-verb algebra below — relocate the primitives the UI never exposes, never drop them.
statement: When a command surface is shaped to mirror a UI's workflows, borrow the UI's workflow VOCABULARY at the top, but keep a uniform `<grain> <verb>` ALGEBRA underneath; the primitives relocate beneath the workflows — they are never removed.
problem: A UI user clicks one state-aware button, so the surface can hide every primitive behind it. An agent does not click — it composes, loops, and discovers — so it needs the primitives the UI never exposes (build a single node, sign a single gate). Shaping the command surface to *mirror* the UI one-for-one amputates exactly those primitives, and a later "make it match the UI" change keeps re-amputating them. The agent and the UI user are different users with different needs from the same surface.
approach: Treat the surface as a SUPERSET of the UI, not a copy of it. The top level borrows the UI's workflow names so the two surfaces teach one mental model; beneath each workflow, keep a stable, uniform grain-verb algebra the agent can compose and loop over, reached by drilling in just-in-time (`asset:pull-based-context-architecture`). When a workflow absorbs a primitive, the primitive MOVES under it and keeps a back-compat alias — relocation, never removal. Workflow names may churn as the methodology grows; the grain algebra underneath stays stable.
tradeoffs: You trade a clean one-to-one map to the UI for a top surface that carries more than the UI exposes and churns as workflows multiply. The payoff: the agent keeps predictable primitives to script against, and the two surfaces still teach the same goals.
provenance: The durable rationale of ADR-0118 (workflow-first CLI surface): the agent-facing `storytree` CLI borrows the studio's workflow vocabulary on top while keeping the grain-verb primitives below. Authored during the ADR-0118 reshape.

title: A relocated CLI verb keeps a back-compat alias
description: When a workflow absorbs a CLI primitive, the old verb keeps a back-compat alias resolving to the same code path — the surface never silently drops or renames a primitive.
statement: Every CLI verb the workflow-first reshape relocates keeps a back-compat alias that resolves to its new home; the surface never silently drops or renames a primitive out from under a caller.
rule: When a workflow absorbs a proof primitive (the build/adopt/witness reshape, ADR-0118), the old grain verb does not disappear — it keeps a back-compat alias that resolves to the SAME code path as its workflow-first home. No caller, script, or agent habit breaks silently; a relocation is a move PLUS an alias, never a removal. This is the surface-level companion to `asset:cli-surface-is-a-superset-of-the-ui` — relocate the primitive, don't lose it.
enforcedBy: The CLI back-compat alias-coverage suite (`packages/cli/src/cli-aliases.test.ts`, run in the package's `pnpm test` and in CI): a growing table of (relocated verb → alias) pairs, each asserting the alias resolves to the SAME envelope as its workflow-first home (a deepEqual over a side-effect-free dispatch), plus a coverage floor that fails if a unit relocates a verb but registers no alias row. A dropped or renamed alias fails the suite; a forgotten registration fails the floor.
failureMode: If the boundary is crossed, a reshape renames or removes a verb without an alias, silently breaking every script, caller, or agent habit that invokes the old form — the exact silent breakage ADR-0118 forbids (its discipline mirrors the `story adopt-plan` redirect).
provenance: Authored during the ADR-0118 reshape to make the 'every moved verb keeps a back-compat alias' promise mechanically enforced, not merely stated.

title: A live-only-provable guarantee is an honesty gap
description: A fail-closed safety or honesty guarantee whose ONLY verification path is expensive, fragile, or billed (a live `--real`/SDK run, a credentialed external call) is itself an honesty gap — between those runs the property goes silently unverified. Give it a cheap, offline red→green that fires the REAL enforcement (not just the predicate behind it) and pins the external contract it stands on, without taking on that contract's cost.
statement: A fail-closed safety/honesty guarantee that is provable ONLY by an expensive, fragile, or billed path is itself an honesty gap; it must also have a cheap, offline red→green that fires the real enforcement and pins the external contract it depends on.
why: The prove-it-gate makes `healthy` mean earned on-disk evidence, but it is silent on whether the guarantee was AFFORDABLE to re-prove. A central safety property whose only test is a live, subscription-billed, auth-fragile run (e.g. the SDK leaf's write-scope under `permissionMode: 'bypassPermissions'`, enforced by a `PreToolUse` hook closure) is one a session will, in practice, stop exercising — too slow, too costly, breaks when an OAuth token lapses — so it silently rots between live runs and a regression lands unseen. Two traps make the gap easy to miss: testing the PREDICATE instead of the ENFORCEMENT (unit-testing the pure `decideWrite` policy while never firing the wired hook closure that actually denies the write leaves the guarantee's real wiring unproven — `test-creation-principles`' deletion test applied to the enforcer, not its helper), and ASSUMING the external contract holds (the hook stands on the runtime's hook/permission types and option semantics; a version bump that renames a field or flips a default breaks the guarantee silently). A live smoke also cannot be a green's basis here (`adr:0099`), so the cheap offline proof is the only one that can both run constantly AND derive trust.
howToApply: When a fail-closed guard's only proof is a live/billed/credentialed path, treat that as a gap and add an offline red→green that: (1) fires the REAL wired enforcement — invoke the actual hook closure / scoped executor / refusal path and assert it DENIES the out-of-scope case and records the violation, while allowing the in-scope case — not merely the pure predicate it calls; verify by a deliberate red (open the wall / smuggle the forbidden tool → the test goes red → revert → green). (2) Pins the external contract it depends on WITHOUT importing the costly runtime: surface the dependency's types at the single sanctioned import site (`asset:one-model-boundary`, ADR-0004) via a type-only re-export, and add compile-time literal pins for the load-bearing option semantics (the permission mode, the absence of a dangerous tool from the allowlist, the matcher) so a dependency bump that changes them fails the gate. The live path stays valuable as a real end-to-end smoke; it just stops being the SOLE proof. Distinct from neighbours: `test-fixtures-mirror-production-failure-modes` assumes a test exists and asks if its fixture is honest; `real-test-must-not-leak-a-handle` is OS-handle hygiene so an offline leg does not hang; `audit-the-signed-verdict` audits an already-run proof's coverage. This rule is upstream of all three — it says the offline proof must EXIST and must fire the real enforcement.
provenance: Graduated 2026-06-27 (ADR-0095) from the unit that closed the write-scope-wall honesty gap in the live SDK leaf (`packages/agent/src/sdk-author.ts` `ClaudeAgentAuthor`): the SDK leaf's entire write-scope + honesty guarantee — a fail-closed `PreToolUse` hook closure plus a Bash-free tool surface, under `permissionMode: 'bypassPermissions'` — had NO offline test firing the wired closure (only the pure `decideWrite` predicate was unit-tested), so the central safety property was verified solely by live, subscription-billed, auth-fragile `--real` builds. The fix added an offline red→green that fires the real closure (out-of-scope/escaping/no-path Write DENIED + recorded, in-scope allowed) and pins the SDK hook/permission contract via a type-only re-export (preserving ADR-0004's single-import-site rule) plus compile-time literal pins (permission mode, no-Bash, `Write|Edit` matcher), verified by a deliberate red→green. Durable essence extracted; the event-record (file paths, PR) stays in git (ADR-0095 D5). Implements ADR-0030 (SDK-as-runtime), ADR-0020 (red-green enforced spine-side), ADR-0004 (one model boundary); composes with ADR-0099 (a live smoke cannot derive a green). Refines prove-it-gate; sibling of test-creation-principles, test-fixtures-mirror-production-failure-modes, and real-test-must-not-leak-a-handle in the honest-test family. Consumers: red-builder, session-orchestrator, frontend-builder.

title: The human witness label is for a judgment gap, never cost
description: A UAT leg is witnessed by a `human` only when its success condition has no compiler (an aesthetic or an owner value call); a machine-observable success that is merely expensive, live, or not-yet-harnessed is `machine`, witnessed by a standing or deliberate spine-signed proof — never `human` standing in for a missing harness.
statement: A UAT leg is witnessed by a `human` ONLY when its success condition has no compiler — an aesthetic or an owner value call that no code can decide (`operator-attested`); a success that is machine-observable but merely expensive, live, or not-yet-harnessed is `machine`, witnessed by a standing or deliberate spine-signed proof, and is NEVER labeled `human` to stand in for a missing harness.
why: The witness glyph is load-bearing information: `human` should mean 'a real person must judge this,' and a machine-observable outcome wearing that glyph lies twice — it hides a checkable fact behind a person, and it makes the crown wait on an operator ceremony for something a signed verdict already proves. The confusion is easy because two very different reasons reach for the same glyph: a genuine no-compiler judgment ('does it look right?') and an un-automated-but-checkable fact ('is the proven commit an ancestor of `main`?'). Cost is the most seductive false premise: the `--real`/`--live` leaf is subscription-funded and spawns against a maxTurns brake, not a paid meter (ADR-0030 / ADR-0130), so 'too expensive to prove live' usually does not even hold — and when a faithful proof genuinely IS live-only, the honest answer is a deliberate spine-signed run (`a-live-only-guarantee-is-an-honesty-gap` applied to the LABEL: a merely-expensive proof still earns a machine witness, backed where it can be by a cheap offline twin), not a human glyph. Let cost, liveness, or a missing harness earn `human` and the label stops carrying honest information — `machine` vs `human` no longer tells a reader whether a person is truly required.
howToApply: When authoring or resolving a UAT leg's witness, ask ONE question: does its success condition have a compiler? If a machine can read the outcome off an exit code, a persisted `events.verdict`, a git fact, or an executable probe — even one that must run live, or that does not exist yet — the leg is `machine`: bind it to the exact command-bearing `observe` gate that witnesses it, and if that proof is inherently live, back it with a cheap offline twin (`a-live-only-guarantee-is-an-honesty-gap`) or, where none can exist, mark the live-only proof the accepted exception and say why. Reserve `human` for a genuine no-compiler judgment — the look/feel of a surface, an owner value call — the `operator-attested` mode an agent can never self-grant. Never reach for `human` because the harness is missing, the run is slow, or the leaf is billed: a missing harness is a BUILD task, not a judgment gap. This is the labeling rule that sits over `uat-proves-the-goal-not-the-surface` — that principle says don't SILENTLY skip a surface an agent cannot exercise; this one decides WHICH witness the recorded gap gets, and refuses `human` as a placeholder for absent automation.
provenance: Graduated 2026-07-11 (ADR-0095) from ADR-0184 decision d.2, which corrected drive-machinery's three live Story-UAT legs (3 The REAL build, 4 Land it, 7 Dogfood) from `human` to `machine`: reviewing ADR-0180's in-passing 'they remain human' assertion surfaced that the legs wore the `human` glyph for a COST/harness reason rather than a judgment gap — and the cost premise was itself wrong (the `--real`/`--live` leaf is subscription-funded, ADR-0030/0130, not the metered Cursor leaf, ADR-0177). Generalizes `a-live-only-guarantee-is-an-honesty-gap` from PROOF ARCHITECTURE (a live-only guarantee still needs a cheap offline twin) to the WITNESS LABEL (a live/expensive/un-harnessed-but-observable success is `machine`, never `human`), and rules out the loose framing ADR-0092 named ('human-witness = cost, surfaced never pretended') by removing cost from the `human` test entirely. Sharpens the witness-label call `uat-proves-the-goal-not-the-surface` leaves loose, and preserves `operator-attested` (ADR-0070/0007) as the genuine judgment gate. The event-record — the per-leg conversions, the new ancestry `observe` gate, the PRs — stays in git and ADR-0184 (ADR-0095 D5). Primarily relevant to the witness-label authors (story-author, session-orchestrator); wiring it into their `stands on` is a deliberate guidance follow-on, not part of this graduation.

title: Desktop E2E conventions
description: How to work on the desktop Electron forest-map E2E specs (`apps/desktop/e2e`): when to write an Electron E2E vs a jsdom/node unit test, the offline `/api/*`-stub + real-input + camera-reset patterns, the separate non-blocking CI job, and why the suite stays on `node --test`.
statement: A session adding or changing a desktop forest-map interaction test picks the cheapest runner that can actually catch the regression — a fast jsdom/node unit test by default, an Electron E2E (`apps/desktop/e2e/*.e2e.mjs`) ONLY for what genuinely needs real Electron Chromium + real OS input — and authors every Electron spec against the shared OFFLINE harness, drives it with real input, and resets the camera between interactions so it stays deterministic.
trigger: A session is about to add or modify a test for a desktop (Electron) forest-map interaction — a node click/selection, a pan, a zoom, a left drawer, the chat dock — or to broaden the `apps/desktop/e2e` suite. (Pure read/layout or plain-state checks need no Electron at all; step 1 is how you tell.)
steps:
1. **Decide the runner — Electron E2E vs jsdom/node unit test.** Write an Electron E2E ONLY for behaviour that genuinely needs real Electron Chromium + real OS input: the pointer-capture node-click regression (Electron retargets a captured pointer’s `click` to the capture element, so a node click ran `clearSelection` instead of selecting — plain Chrome does NOT reproduce it, so a Chrome pass is a FALSE pass), a pan (real `pointermove` deltas past the 10px `DRAG_SLOP`), a zoom (a NATIVE non-passive `wheel` listener React’s `onWheel` can’t model), and anything that needs the real layout engine’s geometry. Everything else belongs in a fast jsdom/node unit test: plain React state, pure lib math, DOM-only geometry. Pin GEOMETRY/BEHAVIOUR, never appearance — colour/pixel/shadow/radius is operator-attested UAT, not a machine verdict (ADR-0070). **Worked example (increment 2):** the chat dock’s fold/expand, drag-resize and bottom-anchored overlay are plain React state with `aria-expanded` + DOM geometry — they were deliberately LEFT to `apps/studio/src/components/ChatDock.test.tsx` (vitest + jsdom) and NOT given an Electron spec, because nothing about them needs real pointer capture (see the note at the top of `apps/desktop/e2e/map-interactions.e2e.mjs`).
2. **(Electron only) Author against the shared OFFLINE harness — never the live DB.** The desktop’s backend sidecar (`electron/backend-entry.ts`, the tsx sidecar of ADR-0119) always opens a LIVE Cloud SQL connection; it has NO json/offline mode, and `STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE=json` is read only by the studio’s own dev/serve servers — it is inert for the desktop. So a spec MUST stub every renderer `/api/*` call with Playwright request routing against a deterministic fixture — `stubApi(win)` + `TREE_FIXTURE` in `apps/desktop/e2e/harness.mjs` — and then `win.reload()` so the stubs are in force from a CLEAN mount (the first window resolves after `loadURL` has already started, so the very first fetches would otherwise race the stubs). `launchOffline()` does both and waits for the forest to settle. GUARD offline-ness: assert the rendered `data-story-id`s equal the fixture’s (`renderedStoryIds(win)` === `FIXTURE_MAP_STORY_IDS`) — if the stubs ever break, the spec fails LOUDLY there instead of silently testing live, non-deterministic data (or rendering nothing in CI).
3. **Drive REAL input on the SVG viewport — `win.mouse.*`, not `locator.click()`.** The node-click bug reproduces only under real Electron pointer capture, so a viewport interaction MUST use real input: `win.mouse.click(x, y)`, or `win.mouse.down()/move()/up()` for a jittered or dragged gesture, or `win.mouse.wheel()` for a zoom. A `locator.click()` on the viewport dispatches a synthetic click that can SIDESTEP the capture-retarget the regression depends on — exactly the false pass the suite exists to stop. Plain HTML is the exception: the two left `<details>` drawers (`.panel-drawer.panel-legend`, `.panel-drawer.panel-islands`) are toggled with `toggleDrawer`, a Playwright locator click on `summary.panel-drawer-head` — that IS real input plus actionability/scroll-into-view, so coordinate clicks are only needed for the SVG viewport.
4. **Reset + settle the camera between interactions; read the transform to tell pan from zoom.** Selecting a node ZOOMS the camera and LEAVES it zoomed even after the selection clears, so re-finding a target in the zoomed view flakes (a fixed-timeout reset failed 3/3 on a loaded box). Reset EVERY interaction with `resetToForest(win)` — a `win.reload()` that discards BOTH the selection and the zoom — then let `waitForForestSettled(win)` poll the `g.world-camera` `transform` until it is byte-stable across two reads, BEFORE reading any coordinate (`findStoryTarget`/`findEmptyPoint`). Reading the transform (`readCameraTransform(win)` → `{ tx, ty, scale, raw }`) is also how a spec distinguishes a pan (translation moved, scale EQUAL) from a zoom (scale CHANGED); `waitForCameraChange(win, prevRaw)` polls until the post-gesture transform lands so the assertion reads the settled value rather than racing the React re-render.
5. **Run it locally, then watch CI.** Locally: `pnpm --filter desktop test:e2e` (the `pretest:e2e` step builds the studio dist + the Electron main first). In CI the SEPARATE `e2e-desktop` workflow (`.github/workflows/e2e.yml`) runs the specs under `xvfb` + headless Electron in the pinned Playwright container; it is PATH-SCOPED (only desktop/studio/packages/lockfile/workflow changes) and NON-BLOCKING (ci.yml’s `automerge` needs only `verify`), so it never blocks a merge — but it MUST stay green: check `gh pr checks <n>` and fix a red `e2e-desktop` even though the merge lands around it. Leave the container’s Electron-binary step intact — Electron’s bundled extract-zip is broken on Node 24 in that image, so the workflow fetches the zip and extracts it with `python3 -m zipfile` (the comment in `e2e.yml` explains why); do not "simplify" it back to the default postinstall.
6. **Test runner: stay on `node --test` for now.** Author new specs as `*.e2e.mjs` under `apps/desktop/e2e/` with the hand-rolled `launchOffline()` + `finally`-`app.close()` over the shared harness. Do NOT reach for `@playwright/test` without re-opening the recorded decision and its trade-offs in **Verification** below — adopting a third repo-wide test runner is a deliberate fork, not a default.
surfaces:
- **Repo** — `apps/desktop/e2e/*.e2e.mjs` (the specs: `node-click.e2e.mjs`, `map-interactions.e2e.mjs`) + `apps/desktop/e2e/harness.mjs` (the shared offline harness: `launchOffline`/`stubApi`/`TREE_FIXTURE`/`resetToForest`/`waitForForestSettled`/`readCameraTransform`/`waitForCameraChange`/the drawer helpers). The jsdom/node unit tests this ceremony routes work AWAY from Electron live beside their components — e.g. `apps/studio/src/components/ChatDock.test.tsx` (vitest) and `packages/*/**.test.ts` (`node:test`).
- **CI** — the SEPARATE, path-scoped, non-blocking `e2e-desktop` workflow (`.github/workflows/e2e.yml`): headless Electron under `xvfb` in `mcr.microsoft.com/playwright`. Offline by construction (it stubs `/api/*`), so it needs NO DB and NO secrets. It is intentionally not in ci.yml’s `verify`/`automerge` graph — promote it to a required check once it is proven stable over a stretch of runs.
- **No DB, no live forest** — the entire point of the harness is that these specs never touch Cloud SQL; the only "data" is the in-file `TREE_FIXTURE`. This is what makes them deterministic on a dev box and runnable in a credential-free CI container.
failureModes:
- **Verifying a forest-map interaction in plain Chrome.** The node-click pointer-capture bug is Electron-ONLY; a Chrome pass is a FALSE pass that cost THREE wrong fixes before the suite was built — bigger hit rects (#487), drag-slop (#489), lazy-capture-alone — until the real fix landed (lazy capture + coordinate hit-test, #490). Drive real Electron.
- **Forgetting the `/api/*` stubs (or the reload).** Without them a spec runs against the LIVE forest on a dev box (non-deterministic) and renders NOTHING in CI (no DB/creds) — a green-looking test that proves nothing. The `renderedStoryIds === FIXTURE_MAP_STORY_IDS` guard exists to catch exactly this; keep it in every spec.
- **`locator.click()` on the SVG viewport.** It dispatches a synthetic click that can sidestep the capture-retarget the regression depends on, so the bug slips through green. Use `win.mouse.*` for the viewport (locator clicks are correct only for the plain-HTML drawers).
- **Reading a coordinate before the camera settles, or without resetting the zoom.** A prior selection leaves the camera zoomed, so stale coordinates miss the target and the spec flakes intermittently (the symptom that motivated the reload-reset). Always `resetToForest` then `waitForForestSettled` before re-finding a target.
- **Writing an Electron spec for something jsdom could prove.** Electron specs are slow (they build the studio dist + the Electron main and launch a real browser) and inherently more fragile than a jsdom render; putting plain React state (the chat dock) or pure lib math (`worldCamera`) behind Electron buys nothing and taxes every run. Route it to a unit test (step 1).
- **Treating a red `e2e-desktop` as harmless because the PR auto-merges around it.** Non-blocking ≠ ignorable: a CI-only fix PR auto-merges before e2e is actually green, so half-fixes accrete on `main` (the #493–#497 sequence). Keep CI-fix iteration in ONE held/draft PR (workflows still run on it), or root-cause it locally in the EXACT container with Docker, and land one correct fix.
verification:
The OFFLINE + deterministic half is self-checking and gated: every spec asserts `renderedStoryIds === FIXTURE_MAP_STORY_IDS`, so a broken stub fails the spec rather than silently testing live data, and the `e2e-desktop` CI job re-runs the whole suite on every desktop/studio/packages change. What is NOT machine-checked — and rests entirely on the session honouring this ceremony — is the ROUTING rule itself: nothing stops someone writing a jsdom-able test as an Electron spec, verifying a forest-map interaction only in Chrome, or using `locator.click()` on the viewport. And because `e2e-desktop` is deliberately non-blocking (not in `automerge`’s `needs`), nothing prevents a PR landing while it is red — until it is promoted to a required check, "watch it to green" is a convention, not a gate.

**Test-runner choice — stay on `node --test` for now (not `@playwright/test`).** The suite is `node --test e2e/*.e2e.mjs` with a hand-rolled `launchOffline()`/`finally`-close per test over the shared `harness.mjs`. The trade-offs, recorded here per ADR-0034:
- _For adopting `@playwright/test`:_ auto-fixtures (launch/teardown would replace the per-test try/finally), built-in parallelism, retries, HTML reports, and the trace viewer — the trace viewer in particular is genuinely useful for debugging an Electron interaction failure.
- _For staying on `node --test`:_ zero new dependency or config; it matches the repo’s two existing runner conventions (`node:test` for packages, vitest for the studio) instead of adding a THIRD; the suite is tiny (2 spec files, ~4 tests) and the harness already engineered the flake OUT (offline + deterministic + the byte-stable camera poll), so retries earn little; and Electron launches are heavy, where serial-by-process is safer for resource contention on a dev box / CI container than parallel workers.
- _Recommendation:_ STAY on `node --test`. Revisit IF the suite grows materially, OR `e2e-desktop` is promoted to a required/blocking check (where retries + the trace viewer start to pay for themselves), OR Electron-interaction debugging becomes frequent. Adopting `@playwright/test` would set a repo-wide third-runner precedent — THAT flip is the ADR-worthy fork and should reserve an ADR when it happens; keeping the status quo sets no new precedent, so no ADR is reserved now.
provenance: Derived per ADR-0034 from the `apps/desktop/e2e` suite — the Electron-only node-click regression and its fix (PR #490, after the false-pass detours #487/#489), the offline harness + CI workflow (increment 1, PRs #493–#497), and the broadened pan/zoom/drawer coverage (increment 2, PR #500). Cites the deciding ADRs for the why; on any disagreement the cited ADRs and the code under `apps/desktop/e2e` win.

title: DB control
description: How a session brings the shared Cloud SQL instance up, checks its state, and stops it — keyless REST db-control, mindful of the fixed nightly sleep window. Read before any --pg / live work that hangs on connect.
statement: Bring the shared Cloud SQL instance up before any `--pg` / live work with `pnpm db:up`, check state with `pnpm db:status`, and leave it running — the keyless REST db-control path (ADR-0063), around a fixed nightly sleep window (ADR-0114).
trigger: A session needs live-DB reachability — before a `--pg` Library write, a `--real`/`--live` build, or the studio backed by the live store — or a query that just failed to connect (often the overnight sleep or a slow cold-start).
steps:
1. **Check state:** `pnpm db:status` — a Cloud SQL Admin REST `describe` (activation policy + running state) over an ambient ADC token; no gcloud, no Python (ADR-0063 retired the gcloud subprocess and its credential-lock cascade).
2. **Bring it up:** `pnpm db:up` — REST `setActivationPolicy(ALWAYS)`, then polls until the instance accepts connections. A no-op if already up.
3. **Leave it running.** Do NOT `pnpm db:down` when you finish (owner call, 2026-06-13: sessions kept stopping it between bursts). `pnpm db:down` (REST `setActivationPolicy(NEVER)`) is for a deliberate cost stop only.
4. **The instance sleeps on a fixed nightly window — 01:00–07:00 Australia/Sydney (ADR-0114):** one Cloud Scheduler job STOPS it at 01:00 and a second STARTS it at 07:00, so it stays predictably up across the working day for the member-facing hosted studio. The old idle-aware 5 h auto-stop (ADR-0015 §5) was removed. A manual `pnpm db:up` still works any time inside the window (a no-op if already up); re-run it if a query can't connect after the overnight stop.
5. **Probe, don't assume — never conclude the DB is unreachable from the environment.** A `db:up`/preflight "unreachable within Ns" at status RUNNABLE is almost always a slow cold-start (can exceed the 420s poll — seen ~21 min after the overnight stop), not a wedge: wait and re-probe. A direct connector `SELECT 1` (via `@storytree/library/store` `createPool`) is the definitive check. The 3307-blocked data socket is a REMOTE-session caveat only (ADR-0063 / ADR-0034): a laptop / direct-network session reaches the DB — PROBE it.
surfaces:
- **Cloud SQL instance only** — `pnpm db:up`, `pnpm db:down`, and `pnpm db:status` toggle/read the instance's `activationPolicy` over the Cloud SQL Admin REST API with a keyless ambient-ADC token (ADR-0021 posture, no key files). No Library, story-tree, or repo state is written — this process only changes whether the shared store is reachable.
- **Not this process:** the studio's in-UI "Start DB" / "Wake the database" button (ADR-0049) drives the same REST path for admins from the hosted surface; it is the studio's affordance, not a CLI entrypoint here.
failureModes:
- **Concluding "unreachable" from the environment.** The 3307-blocked data socket is remote-only; a laptop that reads "unreachable" at RUNNABLE is almost always mid-cold-start — a direct `SELECT 1` connects (seen in ~340 ms once warm while `db:up`'s own poll was still timing out).
- **Running `pnpm db:down` at end of session.** The owner call is to leave it up; stopping it between bursts is exactly the churn ADR-0114's fixed window replaced, and it forces the next session into a cold-start.
- **A session working past 01:00** is stopped mid-work by the nightly window (ADR-0114 loses the old "never stop an active session" property) — re-`pnpm db:up` to continue.
- **Reaching for gcloud** when the REST path errors: the gcloud fallback was retired (ADR-0063 REST-only). A REST/ADC failure now surfaces directly (the build preflight refuses with a reason; the studio handlers answer 502) rather than silently degrading — fix the ADC/REST cause, don't shell gcloud.
verification: The `--real`/`--live` build preflight `ensureLiveDb` refuses to proceed when the instance can't be brought up (a clear reason, not a silent degrade — ADR-0063). A direct connector `SELECT 1` is the definitive reachability check. Nothing, however, asserts that a session left the instance up rather than running `db:down` — the leave-it-running rule rests on the session honouring it.
provenance: Derived (per ADR-0034 §2, reference-don't-restate) from ADR-0063 (Cloud SQL Admin REST db-control, gcloud retired) and ADR-0114 (fixed 01:00–07:00 Sydney sleep window, amending ADR-0015 §5); on any disagreement the cited ADRs win. Backfilled under ADR-0154 (the process tier as a standing projection of the decision log). The probe-don't-assume and leave-it-running operating rules are the CLAUDE.md Cloud SQL guidance and the owner call of 2026-06-13.

title: Launch the studio (local)
description: How a session runs the studio locally — foreground Vite or the detached durable launcher — backed by the live store by default. Read before working on apps/studio or witnessing the world/library/forum.
statement: Run the studio locally either in the foreground (`pnpm --filter studio dev`) or detached and durable (`pnpm studio:up` / `pnpm studio:down` / `pnpm studio:status`), backed by the live Cloud SQL store by default — bring the DB up first.
trigger: A session needs the studio UI running on this machine — to work on `apps/studio/src`, watch the forest world / library / forum live, or stand up a surface to attest — rather than reaching for the hosted members deployment.
steps:
1. **Bring the DB up first:** `pnpm db:up`. The studio reads and writes the live store by DEFAULT (`oq-studio-store-default` → B), so it needs the instance reachable ([[db-control]]).
2. **Foreground (a terminal to babysit):** `pnpm --filter studio dev` — Vite on port 5173, tied to the launching terminal.
3. **Durable background (no terminal to babysit):** `pnpm studio:up` starts the SAME server detached (windowsHide, unref'd); `pnpm studio:status` probes it; `pnpm studio:down` stops it (reaping whatever actually holds :5173, not just the recorded pid). State lives in `apps/studio/.studio.{pid,log}`, so one `studio:up` outlives the launching session.
4. **Offline fallback:** set `STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE=json` for the pre-DB JSON backend (`apps/studio/server/devApi.ts`) — it won't reflect CLI / live-store edits, so use it only for offline UI work.
5. **It recovers in place.** The UI self-reports store health (`/api/health`) and shows a banner with a Start DB button when the live store is stopped — no relaunch needed after a DB idle-stop. `/api/health` also stamps the `code` git-HEAD, so a checkout that MOVED under the running server is observable (the "404 unknown endpoint after a merge" trap) — `studio:down` then `studio:up` to reload.
6. **Not this process:** the hosted members studio (Cloud Run + IAP, ADR-0042) is a separate deployed surface — see [[stage-the-attestation-experience]] for standing an experience up for an owner to attest.
surfaces:
- **The local studio server** — `pnpm --filter studio dev` (foreground Vite) and `pnpm studio:up` / `pnpm studio:down` / `pnpm studio:status` (detached durable) both serve `apps/studio` on :5173. `pnpm studio:up` binds the port once; a second start is refused (it detects the existing listener).
- **Library (live store)** — read and written by default (bring the DB up, [[db-control]]); the `STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE=json` backend is offline-only and does not reflect CLI writes. No story-tree status is written — the studio renders health as a projection of signed verdicts, it does not author it.
failureModes:
- **Foreground `dev` dies with its terminal.** Closing the shell (or losing the session) drops `pnpm --filter studio dev`; use `pnpm studio:up` for a launch that survives.
- **Version-skew after a schema-touching merge** — a detached studio keeps running STALE code, surfacing as "specs is not iterable"; `pnpm studio:down` / `studio:up` reloads it, and `/api/health`'s git-HEAD stamp makes the skew visible.
- **Forgetting `pnpm db:up`** — the live-store studio banners degraded (no live layers) until the DB is up; the in-UI Start DB button recovers it in place.
- **Two studio sessions on the same artifact data** — the live store is one-writer-at-a-time for a given artifact (claims are DBOS-deferred); keep parallel studio sessions off the same artifact.
verification: `pnpm studio:status` probes `http://localhost:5173/api/health` — any HTTP response on :5173 (even a 404 from a plain Vite server) counts as serving, and it surfaces the actual :5173 holder so a stale pid file is visible. `/api/health` stamps the running code's git-HEAD, making a moved checkout observable. Nothing gates that the studio is up — it is an on-demand launcher, not a ceremony step.
provenance: Derived (per ADR-0034 §2, reference-don't-restate) from ADR-0042 (the hosted studio + the standalone serve mode; here the LOCAL surfaces beside it) and the CLAUDE.md "How to run" studio guidance; on any disagreement the cited ADR wins. Backfilled under ADR-0154. The version-skew and durable-background operating rules are recorded studio traps.

title: Launch the desktop client
description: How a session builds and launches the Electron desktop credential-host client (apps/desktop) — the thin native shell that loads the compiled studio bundle, exposes the desktop-only Credentials panel, and keeps runtime credentials in the OS keychain. Read before working on apps/desktop.
statement: Build and launch the Electron desktop credential-host client with `pnpm desktop:start` (the root launcher) or `pnpm --filter desktop start` — a thin native shell that renders the COMPILED studio bundle (feature-gated Credentials panel when `window.desktopAuth` is present) and brokers three independently namespaced runtime credentials in the OS keychain, never source (ADR-0109/0111/0179).
trigger: A session needs the desktop client running on this machine — to work on `apps/desktop`, configure credentials through the panel, exercise the keychain credential broker, or attest the shell's appearance — rather than the browser studio.
steps:
1. **Launch:** `pnpm desktop:start` (the root forwarder) or `pnpm --filter desktop start` — both run the desktop app's `start`, which esbuild-bundles the Electron main/preload (`electron/main.ts` → `dist/*.cjs`; Electron can't run raw `.ts`, so the build MUST precede `electron .`) then launches `electron .`.
2. **It loads studio's COMPILED `apps/studio/dist`** — a build-time artifact, not a workspace import (ADR-0111 §3), so rebuild studio (`pnpm --filter studio build`, or via `pnpm --filter desktop run pretest:e2e`) when the bundle is stale. The shell carries NO source, NO build engine, NO stories, and never imports the agent (ADR-0109 d.2/d.4; ADR-0090 preserved).
3. **Credential hosting is its added job over the browser** — three kinds (`oauth`, `api-key`, `cursor-api-key`; ADR-0177) are configured through the desktop-only Credentials panel in the compiled studio UI when `window.desktopAuth` is present (ADR-0179); the main-process broker stores each in the OS keychain (Keychain / Credential Manager / libsecret), NEVER in `localStorage` or plaintext disk. A raw value may exist transiently in the password input and cross store IPC once; it is never persisted in, returned to, or recoverable from the renderer (ADR-0179). Step-2 wiring feeds the held credential per-build into the main-owned backend sidecar (ADR-0119, now built).
4. **Electron stays out of the gate's critical path** — `apps/desktop` carries no `build` script, so `pnpm -r build` skips it (ADR-0111 §5); packaging, code-signing, and auto-update are deferred concerns, not part of launching it locally.
surfaces:
- **The desktop client** — `pnpm desktop:start` (the root launcher) and `pnpm --filter desktop start` both build (esbuild → `apps/desktop/dist/*.cjs`) then run `electron .`.
- **OS keychain** — the credential broker reads/writes via `@napi-rs/keyring` behind a `KeychainPort`; the renderer configures credentials through `window.desktopAuth` only (boolean status, one-way store) and never recovers a stored value (ADR-0179).
- **apps/studio/dist** — consumed read-only as a delivered build artifact (the declared cross-story `depends_on: [studio]`, ADR-0111 §3). No Library or story-tree state is written.
failureModes:
- **Expecting `electron .` to run the TypeScript directly** — it can't; the `start` script esbuild-bundles main/preload to `dist/*.cjs` first. Running Electron against a missing/stale `dist` is the recorded Step-1 trap.
- **Loading a stale `apps/studio/dist`** — the shell renders an old studio bundle; rebuild studio before launching.
- **The forest-map node-click bug is Electron-specific** — a pointer-capture retarget that plain Chrome does not reproduce (a false pass); verify desktop interactions via the `apps/desktop` Playwright `_electron` real-input e2e, not a browser.
- **Assuming CI exercises the shell** — the real `@napi-rs/keyring` adapter, the panel's real-key round-trip, and the shell's appearance are operator-attested (ADR-0070), NOT run headless; the broker's keychain round-trip against an in-memory `KeychainPort` fake and the panel's injected-fake component tests are CI-provable (ADR-0111 §5 / ADR-0179 §5).
verification: The credential broker's keychain round-trip and the Credentials panel's injected-fake component tests are CI-provable (ADR-0111 §5 / ADR-0179 §5). The real keyring adapter, panel real-key round-trip, and shell appearance are operator-attested (ADR-0070), not exercised in headless CI. Launching the client itself is not gated — it is an on-demand developer/operator action.
provenance: Derived (per ADR-0034 §2, reference-don't-restate) from ADR-0109 (the Electron credential-host thin client — WHAT), ADR-0111 (Step-1 lands as `apps/desktop` + consumes studio's compiled `dist` — WHERE), and ADR-0179 (the desktop-only Credentials panel — HOW operators configure credentials); on any disagreement the cited ADRs win. Backfilled under ADR-0154 — deriving this process forced the missing `desktop:start` root launcher to exist (the originating drift ADR-0154 §Context named), which the coverage gate now keeps honest. The Electron build traps are recorded desktop learnings.

title: Release the public website
description: How a change reaches the live public website: it lands in the separate storytree-web repo whose OWN CD publishes it; the parent repo syncs the forest-world engine and gates the site's grounding, and never triggers the publish. Read before touching web copy or the synced engine.
statement: Ship the public website through the SEPARATE `storytree-web` repo (the `web/` submodule) whose own CD publishes it; the parent repo's role is to sync the forest-world render engine (`pnpm sync:web-engine`) and gate the site's claims/engine drift (`pnpm check:web-grounding` / `pnpm check:web-engine`) — never to trigger the publish from here.
trigger: A change needs to reach the live public site (here.now) — its copy/claims, or the synced `forest-world` render engine it consumes.
steps:
1. **Recognise the split.** The public site is a SEPARATE public repo `storytree-web`, vendored here as the `web/` submodule (ADR-0056), with its OWN CD — a push to its `main` deploys/publishes. The parent repo does not publish; branch web fixes off `storytree-web`'s OWN `origin/main`, not the parent's (frequently stale) pinned submodule SHA.
2. **Engine changes** (the site consumes `forest-world`'s BUILT output, never its source — ADR-0093/0100): sync the artifact into the submodule with `pnpm sync:web-engine`; `pnpm check:web-engine` (in `pnpm gate` and CI) fails on drift between the pin and the source. A `forest-world/src` change SKIPs `check:web-engine` locally without the `web/` submodule but FAILS the PR in CI.
3. **Copy / claims:** each load-bearing factual claim about how storytree works carries a `data-grounds="ADR-NNNN"` reference (a handle, not content — it crosses the public/private firewall safely, ADR-0056); `pnpm check:web-grounding` (in `pnpm gate` and CI) fails when a cited ADR is missing or fully superseded, validated at submodule-bump granularity.
4. **Publish is external and owner-approved.** The actual publish is the `storytree-web` CD (a push to its `main` → here.now) — outward-facing and irreversible, so it is an owner-gated action, NOT triggered from the parent repo. Bumping the parent's submodule pin activates the grounding validation for that web SHA. Do NOT invent or trigger a publish from a session — stand the change up and escalate the publish to the owner.
surfaces:
- **Parent-repo entrypoints (all internal):** `pnpm sync:web-engine` writes the built `forest-world` artifact into the `web/` submodule; `pnpm check:web-engine` gates engine drift; `pnpm check:web-grounding` gates that every cited ADR resolves and is not fully superseded. These run in `pnpm gate` and CI.
- **The `storytree-web` repo + its CD (external, prose only):** the site's copy (`src/data/*.json` via Keystatic), the `data-grounds` refs, and the publish live in the separate public repo — there is no parent-repo entrypoint that publishes, by design (the publish is the external CD, owner-approved). No live-store or story-tree state is written by this process.
failureModes:
- **Working from the stale parent submodule pin** — the parent's pin is frequently behind `storytree-web`'s `origin/main` (seen 7 commits); editing from it re-introduces already-fixed bugs. Branch off web's own `origin/main`.
- **Copy that drifts from doctrine** — the recorded "a person signs off" class of overclaim; `check:web-grounding` fails the next submodule bump when a claim cites a missing or fully-superseded ADR (ADR-0056).
- **A `forest-world` change that skips `pnpm sync:web-engine`** — green locally (no `web/` submodule) but a red PR in CI via `check:web-engine`; an additive scene field is also inert on the site until the website's own `worldSvg.ts` maps it.
- **Triggering a publish from a session** — the publish is outward-facing and irreversible; it is the external CD and the owner's call. Standing a change up for review is fine; pushing it live is not a session action.
verification: `pnpm check:web-engine` (drift between the pinned web engine and `forest-world`'s source) and `pnpm check:web-grounding` (every cited ADR resolves and is not fully superseded) both run in `pnpm gate` and CI — an absent `web/` is a local SKIP and a CI hard-fail (the clone step must have run). The publish itself has no parent-repo gate: it is the external `storytree-web` CD, owner-approved.
provenance: Derived (per ADR-0034 §2, reference-don't-restate) from ADR-0100 (consuming surfaces — the website node backed by its drift gate) and ADR-0056 (the `data-grounds` grounding gate + the separate-repo/own-CD publish model); on any disagreement the cited ADRs win. Backfilled under ADR-0154 — DOC ONLY, no publish triggered. The stale-pin and R3F/engine-sync operating traps are recorded storytree-web learnings.

title: Advertise only the capabilities you mount
problem: A shared frontend gates its UI on an identity/capability response (`me.role === 'admin'`, a `member`/`canWakeDb` flag), NOT by probing each route. When a backend deliberately serves only a subset of the shared API — the thick-local desktop sidecar (ADR-0119) mounts the boot READ set and omits the hosted-only `/api/users`, `/api/uat/attest`, and db-control — but its identity response still advertises the full role (`LOCAL_ME.role: "admin"`), the client renders the controls for the unserved routes (the Members nav/panel, the UAT sign button). Those 404 against that backend, so the surface reads as BROKEN — a red "unknown endpoint", a hung "Loading…" — instead of degrading. The same trap waits for any future edge/offline/restricted backend behind the same client.
approach: Down-claim the identity/capability response to match exactly what the surface mounts: a backend that omits a class of routes claims only the role/flags whose routes it serves (the desktop operator is `member`, not `admin`, on their own machine — `member` unlocks read/comment/chat/build, which the desktop DOES serve). The shared client's existing capability gates (`me.role === 'admin'`) then mechanically hide the unserved controls, and a direct visit lands on the surface's own honest degrade state (`MembersPanel`'s "Admins only") rather than a 404. Pin the claim to the mounted set with a red→green on the identity route (`/api/me` returns `member`, never `admin`). The fix for "the shared UI looks broken on this backend" is ALWAYS to correct the over-claim in the identity response — never to special-case the shared frontend per backend, which re-introduces the surface→surface coupling the re-composition forbids. (There is no generic cross-surface gate that a backend's advertised role spans only mounted routes, which is why this is a pattern to follow per surface, not a deterministic guardrail; the shared read-route organism the ADR-0119 §2 consolidation would extract is the natural home for one.)
statement: When several backends serve different subsets of one shared API behind a shared client, a surface's identity/capability response IS its advertised contract — it must claim only the capabilities the surface actually mounts, so a client that gates UI on that claim degrades honestly rather than rendering controls that 404.
tradeoffs: You trade a surface that claims the full shared role — convenient, and "correct" on the hosted backend that serves every route — for one whose advertised identity is scoped to what it actually mounts, so the same client degrades honestly across every backend. The honest claim is narrower and must be kept in step as the surface gains routes, but it is the only claim the shared client can trust without probing. Companion to honest degradation: `asset:uat-proves-the-goal-not-the-surface` records an un-served surface as a human-witness gap; this pattern keeps a served surface from advertising a gap it cannot back.
