provenance: Graduated 2026-06-27 (ADR-0095) from the unit that closed 'chip 4' of the ADR-0113 thick-client arc: the desktop's thick-local backend (ADR-0119) deliberately mounts no admin-only routes (no `/api/users`, `/api/uat/attest`, db-control), but its `LOCAL_ME` identity constant claimed `role: "admin"`, so the shared studio frontend rendered the Members nav/panel + the UAT sign button — admin-only UI that 404s against the desktop backend — looking broken instead of degrading. The root-cause fix down-claimed `LOCAL_ME.role` from `"admin"` to `"member"` in `apps/desktop/src/backend/boot-read-routes.ts` (+ a red→green in `boot-read-routes.test.ts` asserting `/api/me` returns `member`, never `admin`); no studio source changed — the existing `me.role === 'admin'` gates + the `MembersPanel` "Admins only" degrade then hide exactly the unserved surfaces. Authored as a `pattern` not a `guardrail` because nothing deterministically enforces the claim across the general case — a new omitting surface relies on its author adding the same identity-route assertion (the per-surface red→green is the only mechanical check today). Durable essence extracted; the event-record (the two files, the chip) stays in git (ADR-0095 D5). Composes with ADR-0119 §2 (the desktop serves the boot read set, omitting hosted-only concerns) and ADR-0113 (the thick-local boundary call). Consumers: frontend-builder, session-orchestrator.
description: A backend serving a subset of a shared API must not have its identity/capability response advertise a capability whose routes it does not mount — claim only what you serve, so a shared client degrades honestly instead of rendering controls that 404.

title: The fix/change-drive build shape: does a single-contract drive wisp, and how does a fix land?
stakes: If the in-app orchestrator's bug/change fix path is wired to the synthetic --live smoke (the current routed node dispatch), a spawned fix-drive writes no building event to events.work_event — so leaf-provable FIXES never wisp, even with ADR-0137 fully built, and the headline goal 'wisps honestly show up for the work being done' fails for the most common kind of work (changes/fixes, not whole-story builds).
context: A wisp is a persisted `building` work-event with no terminal verdict inside a 20-min TTL (ADR-0048). In the code today (`packages/drive/src/node-build.ts`, `build-worker.ts`), a persisted building event comes ONLY from a `--real` drive (`--store pg`): `buildNodeReal` (`node build --real`) appends one and runs the node's REAL proof; `story build --real` does the same per capability AND opens an auto-merging PR. The UI/chat dispatch `routedBuildRunner` routes a STORY id to `story build --real` (persists, PR, wisp) but a NODE id to `node build --live` — a SYNTHETIC `add(2,3)` smoke that, per ADR-0099-B, must NOT persist a (forged) green, so it is in-memory and writes nothing (no wisp). ADR-0137 d.1 says the orchestrator spawns the builder leaf to drive a change red→green and 'the spine observes RED→GREEN and SIGNS' — which is the persisting `--real` path, not the `--live` smoke. ADR-0137's own consequences flag this as the residual: 'a real unit-level drive is still a smoke today (ADR-0099-B), so the leaf re-driving a single contract for a fix is itself a build-shape detail to settle when Phase 3 is built.' Two sub-decisions are entangled: (1) the spawned fix-drive must use `node build --real` (real proof, persists → wisp), NOT the `--live` smoke — honest because a REAL drive of an EXISTING contract is a genuine red→green, so ADR-0099-B (which only bars SYNTHETIC persists) is respected; and (2) a single `node build --real` produces a signed verdict + a parked `claude/real/<id>-<run>` branch (ADR-0031), but NOT an auto-merging PR — only `story build --real` opens one (ADR-0136) — so how a one-contract fix actually LANDS is unsettled. This is the concrete enabler for honest wisps on fixes; see `docs/research/wisp-coverage-under-in-app-orchestration.md` (OQ-A).
options:
**A — route the spawned fix-drive to `node build --real` (persist + real proof), land via the human accept-to-land gate.** The leaf drives the existing contract for real (wisps, signs a genuine verdict); the human's accept-to-land (ADR-0108 d.3) promotes the parked branch. Cleanest honesty (real drive persists; ADR-0099-B untouched) and reuses the existing gate, but needs a node-level landing affordance the desktop does not have yet.

**B — escalate a fix to its owning story's Build affordance (`story build --real` → auto-merging PR).** Reuses the already-decided ADR-0136 story-Build path and its PR/merge, so the fix lands like any go-green. But a story build authors/redrives EVERY capability in the story — heavy for a one-line fix, and it muddies accept-provenance (you fixed one contract; it rebuilt the whole story), the exact trap ADR-0136 rejected for node→story escalation.

**C — a node-level auto-merge PR for a single `--real` fix-drive.** A new affordance: a passing single-contract `--real` drive opens its own auto-merging PR (like story build, but scoped to one node). Makes fixes land in one gesture and wisp, but is net-new mechanism + policy (an outward-facing PR per fix) and must not become a forged-green back-door (only a REAL drive, never a smoke, may open it).
statement: When the orchestrator spawns the leaf to drive a single contract for a bug/change, does that path persist a real building event (so it wisps), and how does the resulting verdict land?
description: Under ADR-0137 the orchestrator spawns the leaf to drive a bug/change red→green, but the routed node path today is the non-persisting --live smoke (no wisp) and node build --real persists a verdict + a parked branch, not an auto-merging PR. Settle how a single-contract fix-drive persists (wisps) and lands.
recommendation:
**NARROWED (2026-06-29, still open) by [ADR-0138](doc:decisions/0138-the-wisp-is-a-forced-ci-cleared-story-claim-one-coordination.md §Consequences).** A fix's CLAIM now shows on the map regardless of build shape (the forced story-claim wisp), so the "does a single-contract fix wisp at all" half is settled — it does, via the claim. What REMAINS open (the residual this is narrowed to): how a single-contract fix's signed VERDICT + parked BRANCH actually LANDS — the proving colour and the signed verdict still require the `--real` drive (ADR-0099-B untouched), and a one-contract `node build --real` produces a verdict + a parked `claude/real/<id>-<run>` branch but NOT an auto-merging PR (only `story build --real` opens one, ADR-0136). So the live question is the landing affordance for a single-contract fix-drive (Options A/B/C below). Decide alongside ADR-0108 Phase 3 / ADR-0137.

Lean A: route the spawned fix-drive to `node build --real` so it wisps and signs a genuine verdict (respecting ADR-0099-B — only a synthetic smoke is barred from persisting), and land via the human accept-to-land gate, adding the node-level landing affordance as the small new piece. Non-binding until the owner decides; this is a Phase-3 build-shape fork ADR-0137 explicitly deferred, not a re-decision of ADR-0099-B/0136. Decide it alongside building ADR-0108 Phase 3 / ADR-0137.

title: work hierarchy is disk-canonical
description: Work-hierarchy units — story / capability / contract — are frontmatter-markdown FILES under stories/<story-id>/ in the repo, landed through the PR; they are never authored as --pg live-store artifacts.
oneLine: The work hierarchy (story / capability / contract) lives as frontmatter-markdown files under stories/ — disk-canonical, landed via the PR — while the knowledge tier lives in the live --pg store; author each tier on its own canonical surface.
whatItIs: Authoring a story (or capability/contract) means creating frontmatter-markdown FILES under `stories/<story-id>/*.md` in the worktree, landed through the PR — never a `pnpm storytree library artifact new --pg` write. The work hierarchy is **disk-canonical**: loaded by `loadNodeSpec` (`packages/orchestrator/src/node-spec.ts`), read live by the studio from `<repo>/stories` (`apps/studio/server/apiRouter.ts`, `packages/cli/src/tree.ts`), and guarded by the `stories/` corpus guard (`packages/cli/scripts/validate-corpus.ts`; ADR-0039, which deliberately kept the work-hierarchy units frontmatter-markdown while the knowledge tier went JSON/JSONB). Authoring consequence: a new `--real`-armed capability changes the CLI real-buildable help list, so the `packages/cli/src/node-build.test.ts` snapshot must be updated with it or the gate fails.
whatItIsNot: Not the knowledge/library tier model: the-live-store-is-the-edit-surface (ADR-0023) applies to definitions, principles, patterns, guardrails, processes, and open-questions — which ARE `--pg` — and that line misreads as covering stories. It does not: `library artifact new --pg` validates against `LibraryDoc = Knowledge | LibraryAsset` and schema-REJECTS a story or capability. Open-questions, by contrast, are live-only `--pg` artifacts (a `.strict()` schema with no `status` field — disposition goes in `recommendation`, or retire-with-reason per ADR-0065). One boundary, two halves: knowledge tier → the live store; work hierarchy → disk.
provenance: Graduated 2026-07-03 from agent-memory stories-are-disk-canonical-not-pg (ADR-0095) — a recurring authoring trap with no prior Library home: the two canonical surfaces (knowledge → live store, work hierarchy → disk) were each documented alone, and the missing contrast misrouted a story-author brief to --pg (schema-rejected). The event-record stays in git (D5); this definition carries the durable surface-ownership fact. Complements asset:live-store-is-the-edit-surface (the knowledge tier half of the same boundary). Consumers: story-author (authors the files), session-orchestrator (briefs the spawn).

title: glue
oneLine: Un-asserted code within a story: connective tissue binding proven capabilities into a running whole. It declares no capability of its own (no isolatable red→green contract) and is proven transitively at the story / UAT altitude or by operator attestation — never by its own contract.
whatItIs: **Un-asserted code that lives WITHIN a story.** Every line of code serves some journey, so glue has a home — what it lacks is an **isolatable assertion**. A **capability** is *stated* precisely because it has a provable **contract** (an isolatable red→green); glue has none, so it declares **no capability of its own**. It is the connective tissue that binds a story's proven capabilities into a running whole, and it is proven **transitively** — at the story / UAT altitude when the whole journey runs green, or by **operator attestation** where even that can't reach it cheaply — **never by its own contract** (ADR-0158 D1).
provenance: Derived 2026-07-05 from ADR-0158 D1 (grounded by the owner after adversarial review dropped the earlier R1/R2/R3 taxonomy), the write-up of the 2026-07-04 desktop-chat over-routing incident. The single discipline is the extraction check; "within a story vs between stories" resolves the placement question.
description: Un-asserted code that lives WITHIN a story: the connective tissue binding a story's proven capabilities into a running whole. It declares no capability of its own because it has no isolatable assertion (no red→green contract), and it is proven transitively — at the story / UAT altitude, or by operator attestation — never by its own contract.
whatItIsNot: **Not "between stories."** "Between stories" is a category error: code many stories depend on (shared infrastructure — e.g. the CLI `--`-argv strip that serves every command) belongs to a **foundation / platform story**, within *that* story and depended-on by the rest; code that belongs to no story at all is **dead code**. **Not `operator-attested`** — orthogonal axes for why a machine can't sign a unit: glue has *nothing worth asserting* (structural, connective), whereas operator-attested work has *output only a human can judge* (look / feel / live / spend, ADR-0070). **Not a place to bury logic:** the one discipline that matters is — before you call something glue, check it isn't hiding an **extractable pure function**. If real logic is buried in the wiring, it is not glue: extract it and it earns a contract within the story.

title: Subagent context pull
description: Read before spawning a delegatable subagent or when a subagent needs a ceremony/principle body: the essentials-only prompt + just-in-time CLI pull model (ADR-0156/0161).
statement: A spawned delegatable subagent runs on an ESSENTIALS-ONLY prompt — its own prose (role, authority, workflow shape) plus a one-line FLOOR of rule assertions, the inline ESCALATE-UP escape hatch, and a per-step DOORS map — and PULLS ceremony/principle BODIES just-in-time from the CLI at the step that needs them, never carrying a full ref body at spawn (ADR-0156, unified into the node-keyed context DAG by ADR-0161).
trigger: The moment context must be GOTTEN rather than pushed: the orchestrator (or any manager) spawns a delegatable subagent, OR a running subagent reaches a workflow step whose ceremony/principle body it does not yet hold. This is the pull side of ADR-0156 — guidance is no longer inlined at spawn, so it must be fetched at the step.
steps:
1. **The prompt renders essentials-only — no full ref bodies inline (ADR-0156 §1).** A spawned `.claude/agents/*.md` (regenerated by `pnpm build:agents`, via `renderAgentEssentials` in `packages/library/src/store/render-agent.ts`) carries ONLY: (a) the agent's OWN prose kept verbatim — role, authority boundary, workflow, escalation; (b) a `## Floor` / `## Refuse` list where each `rules`/`antiPatterns` ref is its ONE-LINE ASSERTION plus a `storytree library artifact <id>` pull-hint (never the body); (c) the inline `## Escalate UP…` escape hatch; (d) a `## Doors` per-step map. `storytree agents <name>` renders the same essentials view. Nothing situational (ceremony bodies, principle Why/How) is inlined.
2. **Read the floor inline; pull its rationale only when a rule bites (ADR-0156 §2).** The floor is safe as it stands — an assertion resident + unmissable, backed by a code fence (the prove-it-gate spine, the write-scope hook, CI approval-gated-trunk), NOT by a body read at spawn. So act on the imperative directly; when a specific rule actually applies and you need the WHY, pull that one unit's body: `storytree library artifact <id>`. Do not pull the whole floor "to be safe" — that just re-imports the bloat the essentials render removed.
3. **When blocked or out of scope, use the inline escape hatch — never a pull (ADR-0156 §3).** The escalate-up rung MUST be inline, because you cannot pull the instruction to STOP once you are already past the point of knowing you should. On "this isn't my job" (outside role/authority), "I have no process for this" (no step/ceremony covers it and a pull did not surface one), or "a capability gap blocks me until infrastructure is built" — STOP and hand the situation UP with the reason in your return message, along the ladder specialist → manager (session-orchestrator) → owner. Do NOT improvise a process, force-fit a hollow proof, or silently skip.
4. **Pull a step's context just-in-time by following its door (ADR-0156 §4 / ADR-0161 decisions 1–2).** At a workflow step, follow its door `storytree agents <name> --step <step>`, which serves JUST that step's envelope — the step's outbound refs, derived from the agent's structured step→refs edges (`AgentStepRef` on the agent doc) through the ONE shared `node → next:` emitter (the same envelope kernel every CLI command reuses, `packages/cli/src/envelope.ts`). Then follow the `next:` breadcrumb onward, one node at a time — navigation is the existing ADR-0023 choose-your-own-adventure chain, not re-implemented in the prompt. Repo files (ADRs, stories, source) are pulled by direct `Read` of the brief-named path; Library BODIES (ceremonies / patterns / principles) come from `storytree library artifact <id>` or `storytree agents <name> --step`.
5. **The size/structure gate keeps the prompt thin (ADR-0156 §5 / ADR-0161 decision 5).** `pnpm check:agents` fails the build if a rendered agent file overflows the token budget, if any ref's full BODY appears inline (only assertions + pointers are allowed), if a step→refs entry names no real workflow step or a dangling ref key, or if a `context` ceremony ref is not attached to a step (no just-in-case riders). `pnpm build:agents` regenerates the `.claude/agents/*.md` files from the Library so the on-disk prompt stays a projection, never hand-edited.
surfaces:
- **The agent-render surface (read/render, no state written)** — `storytree agents <name>` renders the essentials view of a spawned subagent's prompt (own prose + floor assertions + escape hatch + doors); `storytree agents <name> --step <step>` serves just that step's envelope (the step's outbound refs, via the shared `node → next:` emitter). `pnpm build:agents` regenerates the on-disk `.claude/agents/*.md` files from the Library (via `renderAgentEssentials` in `render-agent.ts`).
- **The just-in-time BODY-pull surface (read, no state written)** — `storytree library artifact <id>` fetches one ceremony/principle/pattern BODY at the step that needs it (a floor rule's rationale, or a door's ceremony); repo files (ADRs, stories, source) are pulled by direct `Read` of the brief-named path, not through a CLI entrypoint.
- **The enforcement surface (gate, no state written)** — `pnpm check:agents` gates the render's SHAPE: token budget, no full-body inline, step→refs integrity, no unattached `context` ref.
- **Writes NOTHING to the story-tree or the live store.** This is purely a read/render/pull path over the Library and repo; it takes no work-hierarchy claim, signs no verdict, and mutates no artifact state. It composes WITH [[library-edit-ceremony]] (which owns live artifact WRITES) and runs INSIDE a spawn the orchestrator's [[merge-ceremony]] wraps, but overlaps neither.
failureModes:
- **Pushing full ref bodies at spawn (the pre-ADR-0156 bloat).** The measured baseline: each delegatable `.claude/agents/*.md` was ~3–7k tokens, 60–83% of it injected ref BODIES (story-author ~7.2k / 90%, librarian-curator ~7.1k), so the machine did the restating `reference-dont-restate` forbids — and inlined a STALE spawn-time snapshot of guidance the agent barely re-touched (a guidance body pulled in ~3 of 119 librarian runs). `pnpm check:agents` now FAILS a rendered file that inlines any body.
- **A thin prompt that IMPROVISES instead of escalating.** The risk a thin prompt invites — inventing a process, force-fitting a hollow proof, or silently skipping work that is out of scope — is exactly what the INLINE escape hatch (step 3) exists to prevent. Skipping "stop and hand up to the manager" turns a thin brief into a wrong result rather than a clean escalation.
- **Pulling everything up front.** A cold `storytree` pull is ~5s; front-loading every ceremony body just moves the bloat into onboarding latency instead of removing it. Pulls are STEP-triggered — because each situational ceremony fires in a fraction of runs, expected pulls per run stay low. The floor and escape hatch are inline precisely so zero pulls are needed to be SAFE.
- **A dangling step→ref or an unattached `context` ref.** A step→refs entry that names no real workflow step, a ref key that resolves to nothing, or a `context` ceremony ref not attached to any step reddens `pnpm check:agents` (step→refs integrity + no just-in-case riders) — the fence the agent-file surface never had before ADR-0156.
verification:
`pnpm check:agents` deterministically gates the prompt's SHAPE: the token budget (≤6000), no full ref BODY inline (assertions + pointers only), step→refs integrity (every entry names a real workflow step; no dangling ref key), and no unattached `context` ref for an agent that carries a step-map. `pnpm build:agents` regenerates the `.claude/agents/*.md` files, so the on-disk prompt cannot drift from the Library source.

What is NOT machine-checked — and rests entirely on the subagent honouring this process at runtime — is the PULL BEHAVIOUR itself: nothing forces a running subagent to actually follow its door and pull the right body at the right step, or to reach for the inline escape hatch instead of improvising. The gate guarantees the prompt is THIN and correctly-shaped (floor present, doors present, no inlined bodies); it does not — and cannot — guarantee the agent pulls when it should. That half is a convention the agent's own discipline upholds, not a gate.
provenance: Derived (per ADR-0034 §2, reference-don't-restate) from ADR-0156 (subagent prompts are essentials-only; the CLI serves ceremony bodies just-in-time) and ADR-0161 (the Library is a node-keyed context DAG; agent step-nodes and process nodes share one `next:` emitter); on any disagreement the cited ADRs win. Authored under ADR-0154 (librarian-curator owns the `process` tier as a standing projection of the decision log) as ADR-0161 decision 4's charter obligation.

title: Friction earns durable guidance by supporting evidence, not by being reported
description: A friction item's essence graduates only when its evidence SUPPORTS the claimed lesson (not merely exists) and the lesson is irreducible — worth is judged against the anti-slop floor, never counted.
statement: Before any friction item's essence graduates into durable guidance, an adjudicator must clear a justification floor whose load-bearing, non-redundant demand is that the cited evidence actually SUPPORTS the stated lesson — a real command, log, or PR is necessary but never sufficient; it must substantiate the claim, not merely coexist with it. The remaining floor is the existing anti-slop corpus applied unchanged — reconstructible-from-today's-corpus graduates nothing (`asset:` blind-reconstruction / ADR-0024), editing the closest existing artifact is preferred to a new one (`asset:edit-first-curation`), only stateless rules graduate and uncertainty withholds (`asset:stateless-vs-stateful-graduation`), the rule must be discriminatory — naming a concrete future situation where an agent acts differently (`asset:signal-and-noise`), it must have recurred or be structurally certain to recur, and the cheapest durable fix (a tool or code fence over prose an agent must remember) is preferred. Worth is EARNED by supporting evidence plus irreducibility and JUDGED, never decided by any count or threshold.
why: A session retro is structurally self-report: there is no typed event stream to cite, and an eager frontier model manufactures plausible-sounding lessons on demand. The specific failure this floor answers is confabulation atop real evidence — a genuine artifact (a true cold-start log, a real PR) is attached to a lesson it does not actually support (the log 'always db:up first' contradicts ADR-0162's need-gated posture), and a gate that checks only that evidence EXISTS waves the junk through. Left unchecked this rots the durable tier the way judgment-only admission did at production scale (a Mem0 audit found 97.8% of 10,134 LLM-gated entries were junk), and a rotted durable tier erodes trust in every piece of Library guidance a future session reads.
howToApply: The adjudicator reads the friction item's prose and evidence and asks first the discriminating question this floor adds: does the evidence SUPPORT the stated lesson, or does it merely exist alongside it? Trace the citation to the claim — a command output, error, or PR that does not substantiate the lesson (or that a live source contradicts) fails here regardless of being real; for any route that renders into agent guidance, verification is spawned to a read-only checker proportional to blast radius. The rest of the floor is applied by citation, not re-derivation: reconstructible-from-today's-corpus routes to nothing (ADR-0024); an edit to the closest existing artifact is preferred over a new unit (`asset:edit-first-curation`); only stateless rules pass and uncertainty withholds (`asset:stateless-vs-stateful-graduation`); the rule must name a concrete future situation where an agent chooses differently (`asset:signal-and-noise`); a one-off waits for reinforcement; and the cheapest durable fix — a tool or code fence over prose an agent must remember — wins. The verdict is a JUDGMENT recorded as prose, never an arithmetic score: worth is earned by supporting evidence and irreducibility, weighed, not counted. The bar deliberately trades occasional delay of real signal — a genuine but singly-observed trap waits for reinforcement rather than graduating on first sight — for the durable tier's trustworthiness; when uncertain, withhold.
provenance: Authored 2026-07-07 for ADR-0168 inc 5 (the friction feedback loop); the behavioural floor the friction-adjudication process's 7-question gate applies as its RULE (the ordered procedure ships separately as the `friction-adjudication` process, ADR-0034/ADR-0154 — this states the durable truth, not the steps). Its one new assertion is evidence-must-support-not-merely-exist; every other criterion is cited from the existing anti-slop corpus per `asset:reference-dont-restate` rather than re-derived. Consumers: graduation-synthesist and librarian-curator (the adjudicators). Grounded in ADR-0168 D5, ADR-0032 §3/§5 (no numeric threshold ever decides what graduates), and ADR-0024 (the blind-reconstruction test).

title: Friction adjudication
description: The PROCEDURE for draining the friction worklist (ADR-0168 D5): run each routable friction item through the ordered 7-question gate, answer into `routeReason`, and route it to nothing / edit-existing / principle-or-guardrail / process / definition / adr / tool with verification proportional to blast radius. Makes NO new policy — the RULE is `asset:friction-justification-bar` + ADR-0168; on any disagreement the cited ADR wins. Read before adjudicating friction.
statement: Drain a friction item by answering the ordered 7-question gate into its `routeReason` (question 1 — the evidence SUPPORTS the claim, not merely exists — is the anchor), then route it: `nothing` archives with a tombstone, `edit-existing`/`principle`/`guardrail` render into future guidance so they carry MANDATORY corpus-investigator verification, `process`/`definition` land on the reference tier, `adr` is a born-`proposed` ADR that IS the owner escalation (with the friction→ADR alignment check), `tool` is capability work routed to story-author — the drain is aged, bounded (K≈3 oldest routable per pre-merge pass), and fail-closed at a ceiling delegated to `check:friction-drain`, and no count or age ever decides what graduates.
trigger: The pre-merge librarian pass (or a spawned board-drain session at the `check:friction-drain` ceiling) reaches routable friction — an open item at least one session old, filed by a branch OTHER than the current one (the filer never adjudicates its own item — no marking your own homework). The dedicated adjudicator is the `graduation-synthesist` (ADR-0168 D5); the pre-merge librarian pass runs the bounded routine drain and hands the deeper judgment (whole-system view, the ADR route, owner-escalation) to it. This governs HOW each routable item is drained; capture is a different moment (the orchestrator's retro step, `asset:session-orchestrator`), and worth is never arithmetic.
steps:
1. **Run the ordered 7-question gate, answering each into `routeReason`** (ADR-0168 D5 — the RULE and the evidence floor are `asset:friction-justification-bar`, not restated here): (1) **Does the evidence SUPPORT the claim — not merely exist?** the anchor question: a real cold-start log does not support 'always `db:up` first' (it contradicts ADR-0162); for any route that renders into agent guidance, spawn `asset:corpus-investigator` to verify (the blast-radius rule in step 3). (2) **Reconstructible?** would a fresh agent re-derive this from today's corpus just-in-time (ADR-0024, `asset:signal-and-noise`) → `nothing`. (3) **Closest existing artifact — why is an edit not enough?** (`asset:edit-first-curation`; the search and its result go in `routeReason`). (4) **Stateless?** (`asset:stateless-vs-stateful-graduation`) — UNCERTAIN stays open. (5) **Discriminatory?** name the concrete future situation where an agent acts differently. (6) **Recurred — or structurally certain to recur?** one-offs wait for reinforcement (the `reinforcedBy` testimony, never a threshold). (7) **Cheapest fix?** a code fence or tool beats guidance an agent must remember — prefer the `tool` route over prose.
2. **Route by the table (executor → reviewer):** `nothing` → the adjudicator archives (tombstone + `routeReason` — the logged reason is the audit; the item is RETAINED, recurrence re-opens it). `edit-existing` → librarian edits (guidance-curator if it touches the behavioural floor) + **corpus-investigator verify**. `principle` / `guardrail` → **guidance-curator** authors the floor artifact + **mandatory corpus-investigator verify** (renders into EVERY future session — the highest blast radius). `process` → librarian derives it, and it must cite its deciding ADR (ADR-0154, makes no new policy). `definition` → librarian, reference tier. `adr` → the adjudicator drafts a **born-`proposed`** ADR with the alignment check (step 4) — this IS the owner escalation, the owner ratifies. `tool` → capability work routed to `asset:story-author` (agent-side, ADR-0158 discipline); a `spawn_task` chip only when the adjudicator judges owner visibility warranted.
3. **Match verification to blast radius.** Guidance-rendering routes — `principle`, `guardrail`, `edit-existing` — flow through the render pipeline into every future session's guidance, so they carry the MANDATORY `asset:corpus-investigator` verification of the friction's factual claims before anything renders. The `adr` route is contained (born `proposed`, owner-held), so it needs the alignment check, not a render-time gate. Batch adjudication is never 'owner-directed in this conversation', so a friction-born ADR can NEVER be born `accepted` (ADR-0110); it is always `proposed`.
4. **On the `adr` route, run the friction→ADR alignment check** (ADR-0168 D5) before the draft leaves the adjudicator: (a) survey `storytree adr list --load-bearing` and NAME every touched decision in the new ADR's Context; (b) `asset:corpus-investigator` verifies the friction's factual claims against live sources; (c) declare `amends` / `supersedes` / `supersedes_in_part` edges up front (`adr-health` enforces them mechanically); (d) a contradiction with an accepted load-bearing ADR HALTS as an owner escalation — never drafted around.
5. **Keep the drain aged, bounded, and fail-closed (ADR-0168 D4).** Only routable items are eligible (aged ≥1 session, filed by another branch). The pre-merge pass drains the K≈3 oldest routable items — NEVER the whole worklist per merge (it doesn't scale with landing rate; parallel sessions would clobber same-artifact adjudications). The fail-closed ceiling (open-count > N≈12 or oldest-open > M≈21 days) is delegated to `check:friction-drain`, which flips WARN → red and requires a board-drain session — agent-side, it never pulls the owner in. This is QUEUE-HYGIENE only: no count or age ever decides what graduates (`asset:meter-fail-closed-caps-in-real-cost`, the ADR-0130 turn-cap precedent).
6. **Honour the anti-self-sealing clause (ADR-0168 D6).** A graduated friction-born artifact is not sacred: contradicting one with NEW evidence is itself first-class friction (re-filed, re-opening the originating item with the counter-evidence), NEVER non-adherence. Without this line the corpus's own owner-fork-bar discipline would make a wrong graduated rule self-sealing.
7. **Stop at routing / escalation.** Setting `route`/`routeReason` and drafting the born-`proposed` ADR is the adjudication's whole job; the per-route authors EXECUTE (guidance-curator authors the floor, librarian the process/definition/edit, story-author the tool), and ratifying a friction-born ADR is owner-held (`asset:human-owns-the-outer-loop`).
surfaces: **Library (live store, events schema)** — reads the friction tier + comments + agent-memory candidates + the decision log; the adjudicator's one write verb is `storytree friction route --pg` (sets `route`/`routeReason`); the per-route authors write their artifacts through the standard Library boundary (`artifact new|edit --pg`), never this process directly. **Repo/CI** — the `adr` route scaffolds a born-`proposed` ADR via `storytree adr new --pg` (`adr-health` validates its edges); `check:friction-drain` reads the live store where reachable (local gates) and SKIPs in DB-free CI, like `check:agents-sync` — so the standing adjudicator duty, not CI, is the primary drain. **Story tree** — the `tool` route hands capability work to `asset:story-author`; no status is written here. No verdict is signed by this process — the owner ratifies friction-born ADRs through the existing OQ→ADR flow.
failureModes:
- **Taking evidence that merely EXISTS as evidence that SUPPORTS** — the anchor failure (D5 question 1): a real log cited for a claim it actually contradicts (a cold-start log for 'always `db:up` first', which contradicts ADR-0162). Spot-verify; spawn corpus-investigator on any guidance-rendering route.
- **Skipping corpus-investigator verification on a guidance-rendering route** — `principle`/`guardrail`/`edit-existing` render into every future session; a confabulated lesson atop real evidence (the STALE-benchmark hazard, ADR-0168 Calibration) self-reinforces once written. The verification is mandatory, not optional, precisely because the blast radius is total.
- **Minting a friction-born ADR born `accepted`** — batch adjudication is never 'owner-directed in this conversation' (ADR-0110), so a friction-born ADR is ALWAYS `proposed` and the owner ratifies. Born-accepted here would forge the ratification the whole loop routes to the owner.
- **Drafting around a contradiction with a load-bearing ADR** — the alignment check (d) HALTS on a contradiction with an accepted load-bearing ADR as an owner escalation; reconciling it silently in the draft hides an owner fork.
- **Draining the whole worklist per merge** — the bounded K≈3 rule exists so the drain scales with landing rate and parallel passes don't clobber the same artifact; a full-worklist drain per merge re-creates the same-artifact race and the archive-mill risk.
- **Adjudicating your own homework** — routability requires the item be filed by ANOTHER branch and aged ≥1 session; the filer adjudicating its own item defeats the aging fence.
- **Rubber-stamping `nothing` to clear the ceiling (the archive mill)** — the biggest residual risk (ADR-0168 Consequences): if a board session archives to clear `check:friction-drain` rather than to learn, the loop performs instead of learning. The Success-measures tripwires (route-nothing share near 0% or 100%, recurrence not extinguishing) exist to catch exactly this — the adjudicator watches them.
- **Treating a friction-born artifact as un-questionable** — contradicting it with new evidence is first-class friction (D6), not defection; reading it as non-adherence makes a wrong graduated rule self-sealing.
verification: Machine-checked only at the edges (ADR-0168 D7): the `friction new` capture validator (evidence present + concrete, cap-3, refs resolve) and schema `.strict()` fence what enters; `check:friction-drain` fail-closed at the ceiling gates QUEUE HYGIENE where the store is reachable (SKIPs in DB-free CI); `adr-health` enforces the `adr` route's declared edges. The GATE ITSELF — the seven questions, routing, reinforce-vs-new, tombstoning, owner-escalation judgment — is adjudicator judgment, never arithmetic (worth is never a threshold). The corpus-investigator verification on guidance-rendering routes is the adjudicator's own honesty check that the evidence supports the claim before it renders. Recurrence extinction (the routed trap stops re-appearing) is the standing success signal the adjudicator watches; a routed item that keeps recurring means the loop produced bloat, not learning.
provenance: Authored 2026-07-07 as ADR-0168 increment 5 (the friction feedback loop's PROCEDURE). Derived from ADR-0168 D5 (the 7-question gate + the route→executor table + verification-proportional-to-blast-radius + the friction→ADR alignment check) and D4 (aged/bounded/fail-closed drain) per ADR-0034 §2 / ADR-0154 — a `process` makes NO new policy, so the RULE lives in `asset:friction-justification-bar` (the evidence floor) + ADR-0168, and on any disagreement the cited ADR wins. The sibling of the ADR-0095 memory-graduation pass (same judgment, a different signal source, read by the same adjudicator). Consumers: graduation-synthesist (chairs it), librarian-curator (the bounded pre-merge drain). Grounded also in the anti-slop corpus — `signal-and-noise`, `edit-first-curation`, `reference-dont-restate`, `stateless-vs-stateful-graduation`, the ADR-0024 blind-reconstruction test.

title: Arc
description: The initiative overlay (ADR-0183): a named multi-story owner intent tracked through an increment log to a closed end-state — upstream of stories and ADRs by provenance, rendered as "Epic" in the studio.
oneLine: The initiative overlay: a named multi-story owner intent tracked through an increment log to a closed end-state — upstream of stories and ADRs by provenance, containing plans, rendered as "Epic" in the studio.
whatItIs:
The fourth grouping tier ADR-0002 parked, returned as an **overlay, not a tier** (ADR-0183 D1): an `arc` names one owner initiative that spans multiple stories, ADRs, and plans, and tracks it to a closed end-state. Nothing proof-related rolls up to it — the story remains the top proof grain (no verdict, no UAT, no rollup on an arc).

An arc is live-canonical like every non-agent kind (ADR-0023) and ceremony-light by construction: its only authored mutations are slow — intent changes, and entries appended to its structured `increments` landing log at each merge ceremony (date, PR#, outcome — what landed, halted, or was re-planned). That log is the durable residue that survives plan pruning.

Every containment edge lives on the CHILD (D3): a plan is born citing its arc (`arcRef`), and an ADR or story gains an optional, immutable `arc:` provenance stamp at creation (`storytree adr new --arc <id>`; story frontmatter). The arc reveals its plans, stories, and ADRs by derived query (`storytree arc show <id> --pg`), never by editing the arc when a child is born. Upstream means provenance and intent, not authority: the arc never overrides story-author on WHAT or the decision log on WHY.

The studio displays the kind as **"Epic"** (a display alias only — the kind key, CLI, and refs use `arc` exclusively).
whatItIsNot: Not a proof unit — nothing rolls up to it; health is judged at the story (ADR-0010). Not durable guidance — lessons still graduate out through ADR-0095/ADR-0168; an arc holds state and pointers only, and implementation surface is banned on it (ADR-0183 D4 — surface lives in anchored, disposable plans). Not a `plan` — the plan is the disposable choreography for ONE increment of an arc. Not mandatory — one-session, one-unit work stays arc-less (D6): the trigger is work that spans sessions or parallel lanes.

title: Plan
description: The disposable, git-anchored execution choreography for one increment of an arc (ADR-0183): the first ephemeral, Postgres-only kind — freshness-checked at consumption, consumed once, then retired.
oneLine: The disposable, git-anchored choreography for one increment of an arc: unit decomposition with proof routes, dependency order, parallel lanes, budgets, and traps — freshness-checked at consumption, consumed once, then retired.
whatItIs:
A `plan` (ADR-0183 D2) hands one arc increment from planning intelligence to executing sessions as an inspectable artifact: the unit decomposition (story/capability ids, each with its proof route — `--real` red→green, glue per ADR-0158, or operator-attested), dependency order, parallel lanes (which units are independent, expected file surface per lane as fence hints, contention warnings), budgets in turn-cap vocabulary (ADR-0130), known traps, and escalation points.

It is the first EPHEMERAL kind (`EPHEMERAL_KINDS` in `packages/library`): live-store-only, never in `knowledge.json`, ignored by `export-corpus`, `sync-corpus`, and the `check:corpus-sync` gate. Born citing its arc (`arcRef`, D3) and pinned to the git commit it was planned against (`anchor.sha`). Consumption begins with a mechanical freshness check — `storytree plan check <id> --pg` git-logs the paths the plan names since its anchor; drift past threshold means re-plan, not repair. Once consumption starts a plan is never edited — re-planning is cheap by construction, so supersede (`status`: draft → ready → consumed / superseded / retired). Consumed plans are prunable; the owning arc's increment log is what endures.

Authored by the `planner` agent (D5); consumed by the session-orchestrator, which validates freshness, takes lanes via the existing claim machinery (ADR-0121/0142), executes, and records the outcome on the arc.
whatItIsNot: Not durable — no git history, no seed, prunable once consumed; anything worth keeping graduates to the arc's increment log or the Library. Not a decision or a work definition — a design fork discovered while planning exits to an ADR, and hierarchy changes route through story-author; a plan only choreographs work that is already defined. Not mandatory — a one-session, one-unit piece of work stays planless (D6); mandatory plans would be plan-theater. Not reachable from remote (web/VM) sessions — plans are live-store data behind the 443-only egress block (ADR-0063).

title: Implementation surface is written only into anchored, disposable artifacts
description: File lists, package paths, and edit footprints rot fast: they may only live in artifacts that pin the git SHA they were true at and are superseded (never repaired) on drift — a durable doc carries intent and pointers only.
statement: Implementation surface — file lists, package paths, expected edit footprints, ordering over concrete code — may only be written into artifacts that are both git-ANCHORED (pinned to the commit they were authored against, freshness-checked mechanically at consumption) and DISPOSABLE (superseded on drift, never repaired); a durable artifact — an ADR body, story, principle, definition, or arc — never carries it (ADR-0183 D4).
why: Surface data is the fastest-rotting prose in the corpus: files move, packages dissolve, and a stale file list misleads silently — the reader cannot tell truth from rot (the exact failure ADR-0139 evicts execution prose from ADR bodies for). In an anchored artifact the same list is honest: `anchor.sha` makes staleness DETECTABLE — git-log the named paths since the anchor, the proof tier's source-drift move applied to intentions — and disposability makes the response cheap: re-plan, don't repair.
howToApply: About to write a file path, package list, or edit footprint into a durable doc? Move it into the anchored disposable tier — today a `plan` (ADR-0183), checked by `storytree plan check <id> --pg` — and leave the durable doc with intent, outcomes, and pointers. The test: would this list still be true in a month, and would the reader notice if it weren't? When a durable doc must name code (a guardrail's enforcement point, a techstack constraint), name the owning SURFACE — a command, a package, a gate — never an enumerated file list.

title: planner
description: The plan author (ADR-0183 D5): expensive-tier planning intelligence that reads an arc and the current tree and writes ONE git-anchored, disposable plan for the next increment — it choreographs, and never decides or defines work: design forks exit to an ADR, hierarchy changes route to story-author.
oneLine: The plan author (ADR-0183 D5): expensive-tier planning intelligence that reads an arc and the current tree and writes ONE git-anchored, disposable plan for the next increment — it choreographs, and never decides or defines work: design forks exit to an ADR, hierarchy changes route to story-author.
role: The separable planning half of the model-tier economics (ADR-0183): planning intelligence and execution intelligence are different spends, so an expensive planner authors the choreography as an inspectable artifact that cheaper orchestrating sessions consume. Given an arc (the owner's initiative), it studies the arc's increment log, the work hierarchy, the decision log, and recent movement on the surfaces involved, then authors ONE `plan` for the next increment: unit decomposition with proof routes, dependency order, parallel lanes with fence-hint file surface, budgets in turn-cap vocabulary (ADR-0130), traps, and escalation points. The plan is the handoff contract — reviewable before any `--real` build burns turns, reusable across N parallel sessions each taking a different lane. It authors plans and NOTHING else: it never decides (a design fork discovered while planning is surfaced, not settled), never defines work (story/capability changes route through story-author), and never executes.
outcome: One valid `plan` document upserted to the live store per invocation — born citing its arc (`arcRef`), anchored to the commit it was planned against (`anchor.sha` + date), body complete (objective, decomposition with a proof route per unit, lanes when parallel, budgets and traps when known), `status` left `draft` for review or flipped `ready` when the spawning session asked for a consumable plan. Zero writes anywhere else: no code, no stories, no ADRs, no arc edits (the increment log is appended by the landing session, not the planner).
tools: Read-only repo access (Read/Grep/Glob; `git log` / `git rev-parse` to anchor the plan and to check recent movement on the surfaces it names); Library CLI reads (`storytree library …`, `storytree tree`, `storytree adr list`, `storytree arc show <id> --pg`); exactly one write surface: `storytree library artifact new --file <plan.json> --pg` (plans are live-only — no DB, no plan). Least-authority: nothing else.
workflow:
**Session start.** Confirm the target arc and that the live DB is reachable (plans are live-only; no DB → stop and report). Pull the arc and its derived children (`storytree arc show <id> --pg`) and read the increment log — what landed, what halted, what was re-planned.

1. **Scope one increment** — the minimum coherent next step toward the arc's end state (slow growth), sized to hand off.
2. **Decompose into provable units** — by the routing filter ("does this piece have an isolatable red→green test?"): `--real` red→green, glue (ADR-0158), or operator-attested; name each unit's story/capability id and order by dependency.
3. **Declare the lanes** — which units are independent, the expected file surface per lane as fence hints for the takers, and where lanes contend.
4. **Budget and traps** — turn-cap vocabulary (the default 16; name the fiddly modules that need more, ADR-0130), known traps on this surface, and the points where the executor halts for the owner.
5. **Anchor** — pin `anchor.sha` to the commit planned against (current `origin/main` HEAD) with today's date.
6. **Write the plan** — `storytree library artifact new --file <plan.json> --pg`, then stop. One plan per invocation; the orchestrator consumes it (freshness check → claim lanes → execute → append the arc increment at landing).

**Stop condition:** the plan is written, or a blocker is surfaced. A design fork or hierarchy gap discovered mid-plan is recorded as an escalation point (or halts the plan when load-bearing) — never settled inline.
escalation: A design fork worth an ADR: name it in the plan's escalation points and surface it to the spawning session — the planner never runs `adr new` or settles it. A gap in the work hierarchy (a unit with no story/capability home): route to story-author. An arc whose intent no longer matches the tree, or an increment that cannot be decomposed into provable units: surface to the owner via the session. DB unreachable: stop — a plan that cannot persist is never parked on disk as a workaround (plans are ephemeral live-store data, ADR-0183 D2).
